title: TrapLedger: Intelligent Deception and Immutable Logs for Cyber Defense
authors: Mohammad Sayduzzaman, Fatin Anjum Adnan, Waliullah Topu, Md. Rejwan Islam Rana, Md Tanjum An Tashrif, Tawhidur Rahman
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Advancements in Computational Sciences (ICACS)
abstract: With the passage of time cyberattacks are getting more complex and identification of recent cyberattacks need more innovative and well arranged planning and machine learning based monitoring specially in the fields like threat detection, log integrity and forensic examination. Existing honeypot systems are facing challenges in real time log processing, tracking and ensuring immutability. It was started with the static honeypots and very basic ELK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) base monitoring during 2018 to 2021 with the aim of adaptive and intelligent deception. At 2024 Blockchain integrated and AI driven honeypot make the break throw with its efficient rreal time detection, tamper proof logging along with simple and dynamic deployment. This paper employs a TPot honeypot server to simulate vulnerable services, a syslog server with AAA (Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting) protocols for secure log aggregation, and the ELK stack for real time log parsing and visualization. Long term, tamper proof event storage is achieved through a blockchain log server. Data are collected via rsyslog, rendered in Kibana dashboards, and trigger real time alerts during malicious activity. Significant alerts and logs are stored in a bespoke blockchain ledger, guaranteeing integrity and digital forensic preparedness. Using predefined ELK alerting rules help us to achieve 99.71% accuracy, 1.00 precision, and 99.59% recall, which outlines the suggested system's acceptability and resilience. It can also be regarded as the foundation of a cyber defense that is ready for forensic analysis.

title: AI Accelerator Based on Uniformly Segmented Linear Regression for Real-Time Diagnostics and Wearable Health Monitoring Systems
authors: Dat Ngo, Hung T. Nguyen, Tho V. Nguyen
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
abstract: AI inference in healthcare frequently operates on edge devices with limited computational resources, where efficient arithmetic operations are essential. Traditional multiplication and division in digital signal processing often become performance bottlenecks. The logarithmic number system (LNS) offers a promising alternative by transforming multiplication and division into simpler addition and subtraction operations. However, many existing LNS methods sacrifice accuracy, making them unsuitable for high-precision medical applications. This paper presents a high-precision technique for computing binary logarithms and antilogarithms using uniformly segmented linear regression, specifically designed to accelerate AI inference in healthcare scenarios. The method decomposes inputs into integer and fractional parts, segments the fractional domain based on fixed most significant bits, and applies linear regression within each segment to approximate logarithmic and antilogarithmic functions. Experimental results show that the proposed approach outperforms benchmark LNS approximation methods in accuracy, making it a viable core component for AI accelerators in real-time diagnostics and wearable health monitoring systems.

title: Cybersecurity Threat Detection and Mitigation in Islanded Microgrids
authors: D Sarathkumar, S Arun Kumar, Sathesh Kumar Thirumalaisamy, M. Geethalakshmi, D Nageswari, S Rajesh, S. PrinceSamuel, Murugesan Manivel
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The increasing adoption of islanded microgrids as a resilient solution for localized power generation and distribution has introduced new cybersecurity vulnerabilities. These systems, often integrated with advanced communication and control technologies, are exposed to sophisticated cyber-attacks that can compromise their stability, reliability, and safety. This paper presents a comprehensive study on cybersecurity threat detection and mitigation strategies tailored for islanded microgrids. The research categorizes potential cyber threats, including denial-of-service (DoS), false data injection (FDI), and malware-based attacks, and analyzes their impacts on the operational integrity of microgrids. Advanced threat detection techniques, leveraging machine learning algorithms, intrusion detection systems (IDS), and real-time monitoring frameworks, are explored to enhance situational awareness and rapid anomaly identification. Furthermore, the paper proposes mitigation methodologies focusing on network segmentation, resilient control design, and cryptographic security protocols to safeguard the microgrid from both internal and external threats. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed detection and mitigation approaches in maintaining system stability under cyber-attack scenarios. The findings underscore the critical need for a multi-layered cybersecurity framework to ensure the secure and reliable operation of islanded microgrids.

title: Non-Contact Breathing Rate Monitoring Using an RGB Camera
authors: Ngo Thuy Duong, Tran Mai Linh, Pham Manh Hung
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
abstract: Monitoring breathing rate (BR) is an important measurement for the diagnosis of diseases and the early intervention of life-threatening conditions, which then provides appropriate treatment to increase patient life expectancy. To limit the skin irriation from contact-based methods, this study presents a low-cost, non-contact BR monitoring using an RGB camera to record chest movements caused by respiratory activity, which adopts anatomy-based selection of the chest region of interest (ROI) and signal processing to address motion sensitivity. While the existing optical approaches still lack thorough evaluation of optimal chest ROI and often overlook BR estimation over time, the system includes: (i) detection and tracking of the chest region exhibiting the most prominent respiratory motion, (ii) extraction of the breathing signal from image sequences based on pixel intensity change, (iii) elimination of breathing-unrelated events from the signal, and (iv) estimation of the BR value every second. The performance of the system was evaluated in seven healthy volunteers wearing casual clothing, considering ROI selection for chest movements as an influencing factor. Reference data were obtained by an ADInstruments BR measurement device using an abdominal belt respiration sensor. The results show that the system achieved higher precision when ROI was restricted to the central upper chest, giving a mean absolute percentage error of 5.38 %. In the range of 6 to 100 breaths per minute (bpm), the mean difference between the proposed system and the reference device was <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-0.63 \pm 4.48 \text{bpm}$</tex>, with subjects at 50 cm from the camera.

title: The ResNet50-Based Framework for Accurate Identification for Orange Fruit Diseases
authors: Seerat Singla, Sripelli Jagadish, Manisha Aeri, Aanchal Rawat
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The cultivation of orange is crucial in citrus industry in the world and its output and commercialization is largely hampered by numerous diseases. Such diseases can be identified accurately and early which is key to the sustainability of agricultural practices. The paper introduces a machine learning-based method of the automated classification of orange fruit diseases based on the Orange Fruit Dataset retrieved in Kaggle and consisting of 8,600 images belonging to three separate categories Citrus Canker, Healthy, and Melanose. The model was tuned with custom dense layers and was trained on stratified sampling with $80-10-10$ split of training, validation, and testing, respectively. Preprocessing and data augmentation methods were used. The classification metrics used to evaluate the performance of the model are accuracy, precision, recall and the F1-score. The suggested ResNet50-based model had a high overall accuracy of 97, and a precision of 0.97, recall of 0.97 and F1-score of 0.97 showing high and credible performance on all three disease classes. These findings validate the usefulness of the model in automated diagnosis of diseases in intelligent agricultural systems.

title: Turbulence Modeling in Aerospace Flows: A Data-Driven Approach Based on Modified Multilayer Perceptron
authors: Rongjie Qiang, Dapeng Xiong, Hongbo Wang, Taiyu Wang, Yu Zeng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Aerospace Engineering (ICMTAE)
abstract: Turbulence modeling remains a pivotal challenge in aerospace fluid mechanics, where conventional Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) models frequently yield substantial inaccuracies in intricate flow regimes. This paper introduces a data-driven turbulence modeling methodology employing a modified multilayer perceptron (ModifiedMLP) neural network to forecast Reynolds stress tensor components (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\tau_{x x}, \tau_{y y}, \tau_{x y}$</tex>), tailored for aerospace flow simulations. Drawing upon a synthetic CFD dataset comprising 5000 turbulent samples, our approach adapts Bayesian deep neural network techniques by integrating Dropout (0.1) and L1 regularization (weight_decay=1e-5) to bolster generalization and counteract overfitting. The experimental setup utilizes the Adam optimizer (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\operatorname{lr}=0.001$</tex>), spanning 300 epochs to maintain CPU execution below 3 minutes. Outcomes reveal model <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> scores ranging from 0.964 to 0.999 and MSE below 0.007, surpassing baseline linear regression (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2} \sim 0.998$</tex>, albeit with inferior generalization). Comprehensive visualization analyses—encompassing learning curves, scatter plots, heatmaps, vector fields, contour plots, and t-SNE embeddings—elucidate flow modalities and feature salience (e.g., dudx <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.993$</tex>). Cross-validation attains <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2} \sim 0.998$</tex>, while subset performance reaches <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=0.962$</tex>. This efficient, robust framework is apt for simulating aircraft wing turbulence, augmenting precision in aerospace design. Prospective expansions to 3D high-Re flows are explored.

title: Generative Imputation and Graph-Neural-Network-Based Classification to Improve Mortality Prediction on Electronic Health Record Data
authors: Tran Phuc Nhat Mai, Nhat Tan Le
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
abstract: Predicting ICU-based mortality using electronic health record (EHR) data faces significant challenges due to missing data, which can negatively affect model performance. To address this issue, this study explores the use of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), specifically the Generative Adversarial Imputation Network (GAIN), for imputing missing values in tabular data, such as the MIMIC-III dataset. The GAN framework includes a generator that learns to fill in missing components based on observed data and a discriminator that distinguishes between real and generated values. In parallel, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are employed for mortality prediction, where patients are represented as nodes connected by edges based on cosine similarity between feature vectors. The GNN model uses Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) to aggregate information from neighboring nodes and extract meaningful features for classification. Experimental results show that GAN-based imputation significantly outperforms traditional methods, improving accuracy, AUC, and F1-score by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5-10 \%$</tex>. Additionally, GNNs demonstrate strong performance in handling class imbalance, with AUC increasing by up to 25% and F1-score by as much as 65%. Statistical analysis confirms that the imputation process preserves the original data distribution, ensuring reliable and robust predictions. Overall, the study highlights the effectiveness of combining GANs and GNNs for improving predictive modeling in ICU mortality prediction under conditions of missing and imbalanced data.

title: AI Enhancing Communication: Sign to Text and Voice Recognition for Hearing and Speech Impaired
authors: S. Sambooranalaxmi, K. Gnanasubramaniyam, G. Karthikraja, B. Micheal Paul Deva Asir, S Jasmine, D Sarathkumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: This paper discusses the advances that artificial intelligence has done to better augment communication among deaf, hard of hearing, and speech-impaired communities. Sign language is converted into text using sophisticated computer vision techniques powered by deep learning architectures that recognize as well as decode sign language gestures through intelligent recognition frameworks in real-time. The system thus converts hand movements, facial expression, and body posture into synthesized speech or text that creates interaction between deaf, hard-of- hearing, and their non-signing counterparts. The AI-enabled voice recognition systems run natural language processing and models to interpret different speech patterns, such as those associated with dysarthria, stuttering, or aphasia, into intelligible forms of speech or text. These amaze with prioritization of seamless communication with education, employment, a public platform thereby making accessibility and inclusivity a priority. Key improvements include adaptive feedback mechanisms that update recognition accuracy through continual user interaction and interface customizations based on top models by employing machine learning, thus maintaining real-time with minimal latency to ensure natural and smooth interaction. However, certain challenges remain: high accuracy across different signed languages and regional dialects, variations in spoken patterns, data privacy, and affordable technologies. Ongoing academic research is directed at training datasets providing cross- linguality, federated learning against the backdrop of privacy- preserving AI models, and low-cost hardware implementations to address the problems the hearing or speech disability community faces in getting empowering tools for independence and social acceptance.

title: Beam Structured DL Precoder Design for Massive MIMO Multiple LEO Satellite Communication
authors: Ziyu Xiang, Ding Shi, Wen-Jie Zhu, Feng Zhu, Xiqi Gao, Xiang-Gen Xia
abstract: In this paper, we investigate the downlink (DL) precoder design for massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) multiple low earth orbit (LEO) satellite communication (SATCOM). We first establish the DL beam based channel model for massive MIMO multiple LEO SATCOM systems by using sampled array response vectors. We propose a closed-form statistical channel state information (sCSI) based space domain DL precoder design for multi-satellite systems by maximizing the average signal-to-leakage-plus-noise ratio (ASLNR). Then, with the proposed beam based channel model, we transform the design of space domain DL precoder into that of lower-dimensional beam domain vector and the resulting space domain precoder is beam structured. Moreover, by leveraging the properties of the beam matrix, we propose a low-complexity design and implementation for the beam structured DL precoder. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed beam structured DL precoder can achieve near performance to the space domain approach with significantly reduced computational complexity.

title: Biomaterial Based Transport Layer for Organic Light Emitting Diodes
authors: G. Mbarki, D. Buso, H. El Housseiny, C. Renaud, M. Khalfaoui, M. Ternisien
abstract: As the demand for more sustainable electronic devices grows, there is an increasing interest in using biobased materials in OLEDs. These greener alternatives offer environmental benefits but often face challenges in terms of stability, performance and durability. This paper focuses on integrating β-Carotene as a biobased transport layer in a standard organic light-emitting diode (OLED) structure (ITO /NPD /Alq3 /Ca). In order to accomplish our objective, the study involves quantum calculations to determine some structural properties (frontier molecular orbitals; E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">HOMO</sub> and E<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">LUMO</sub>), absorption and emission spectra as well as charge carrier mobility calculations, drawing on Marcus theory. These optoelectronic parameters were then incorporated into electrical simulations, using the VictoryDevice model via SILVACO TCAD software, to optimize the device structure and enhance its performance. This analysis has improved our understanding of the optical behavior and charge carrier transport mechanisms of the biomolecule in question and even of the OLED studied structures. As a result, a new OLED structure with better performance (L<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</sub> = 6200 cd/m² and 0,988% of EQE) than the reference structure (L<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</sub> = 6000 cd/m² and 0.94% of EQE), in the same range of the excitation current, was successfully elaborated. Furthermore, we investigated, experimentally, how the purity rate of the biomaterial in question influences the performance of our OLEDs, which led us to notice a drop of more than 85% in the device performances just by using β-Carotene with a purity of 93% rather than the one with 97% of purity.

title: High-Selectivity Frequency-Selective Absorber With Flat Tunable Reflective Band
authors: Shen Meng, Qingxin Guo, Zengrui Li, Yongtao Jia, Ying Liu, Shuxi Gong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A tunable reflective frequency-selective absorber is proposed. It comprises a lossy array and a lossless array, and the resonant frequency of both can be tuned by controlling the reverse-biased voltage of embedded varactors. A pin-header-based bias network is introduced to provide both electrical connectivity and structural support across layers with minimal impact on electromagnetic (EM) performance. By carefully selecting varactors and designing the bias network, the required capacitance variations are effectively controlled. The structure demonstrates a sharp transition between the reflective and absorptive bands, achieving high selectivity alongside a flat reflective band and broadband absorption. Simulated and measured results exhibit great agreement, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed design. The reflective frequency-selective absorber achieves a voltage-controlled flat reflective band from 3.51–4.76GHz to 4.21–5.19 GHz. Outside the reflective band, the structure maintains a wideband absorption spanning 2.2–11.6 GHz.

title: Language as a Bridge: Semantic-guided Cross-modal Gait Recognition via Text Prototype and Feature Decoupling
authors: Zhiyang Lu, Wankang Zeng, Ming Cheng, Cheng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Gait recognition aims to identify individuals based on walking patterns in a long-range, contactless manner. While camera-based methods have advanced significantly, their performance deteriorates under poor lighting conditions. LiDAR offers a promising alternative by capturing accurate 3D gait information regardless of illumination. However, effectively integrating heterogeneous data from diverse sensors, such as LiDAR and cameras, remains a key challenge for cross-modal gait recognition. Existing approaches often minimize modality discrepancy directly, which can lead to class collapse and damage to inter-class discriminability. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Semantic-Guided Cross-modal Gait recognition framework, SG-CrossGait, that introduces text features as the prototype space to bridge camera and LiDAR modalities. We design structured Gait Description Factors (GDF) and leverage multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for automatic factor annotation and text generation, enriching existing datasets with textual descriptions, yielding SUSTech1K-Text and FreeGait-Text. A CLIP-based pipeline aligns multi-grained representations from both modalities to the text prototype space. We further propose the Dual-stream Cross-attention Fusion (DCF) module for fine-grained feature integration and the Semantic-Guided Feature Decoupling (SGFD) module to disentangle shared and modality-specific features. A Multi-task Training (MT) scheme incorporating Gait Attribute Recognition (GAR) further enhances intra-class compactness. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our approach. On SUSTech1K-Text, our method achieves 61% accuracy in LiDAR-to-Camera recognition, outperforming the state-of-the-art method by 8.3%. We also release the Gait-Text benchmark to promote future research at the intersection of gait analysis and vision-language learning. Code and datasets are available at: https://github.com/O-VIGIA/SCCG.git.

title: Integrating Sentinel-1 ETAD into Standard PSI Processing: A Cost-Effective Approach to Phase, Deformation and Localization Accuracy
authors: Mengshi Yang, Hao Wu, Yuxiao Qin, Ling Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) is widely used for surface deformation monitoring, but its accuracy is often limited by atmospheric path delays, orbit errors, and timing inconsistencies. To address these issues, the European Space Agency (ESA) released the Sentinel-1 Extended Timing Annotation Dataset (ETAD), providing refined timing corrections that account for instrument, atmospheric, geophysical, and Doppler-related effects. This study introduces PSI+ETAD: a sensor-native PSI processing framework that fully exploits ETAD’s range- and azimuth-direction timing annotations to jointly enhance interferometric phase quality, deformation time-series accuracy, and PS 3D localization precision — thereby delivering simultaneous, cost-effective improvements across all three pillars of operational PSI. Experimental results show that ETAD integration substantially improves phase quality, with reductions of 60.77% in the Sum of Phase Differences (SPD) and 40.3% in the Phase Standard Deviation (PSD). In deformation velocity estimation, PSI+ETAD achieves a standard deviation of 5.26 mm/year, compared with 18.64 mm/year for PSI (uncorrected), 2.82 mm/year for PSI+STF (PSI with spatio-temporal filtering), and 6.34 mm/year for PSI+GACOS (PSI with GACOS-based tropospheric delay correction), providing a better balance between noise suppression and signal preservation. In time-series analysis, ETAD corrections lower the Standard Deviation of Displacements (SDD) to 3.65 and the Spatio-Temporal Consistency (STC) to 2.23. Validation against GNSS confirms that PSI+ETAD yields the closest agreement with ground-truth deformation, with the lowest errors (MAE of 3.18 mm and RMSE of 3.91 mm). For geolocation, mean offsets of 5.566 m in range, 1.736 m in azimuth, and 0.974 m in cross-range are corrected, with improvements validated against airborne LiDAR data. These findings demonstrate that integrating ETAD into standard PSI workflows provides a cost-effective and operationally feasible correction strategy, significantly enhancing phase stability, deformation accuracy, and geolocation precision for large-scale InSAR monitoring in urban and tectonically active regions.

title: Design and ASM-Based Modeling of Wideband Distributed Downconverter GaN HEMT MMICs up to D-Band
authors: Cristina Maurette-Blasini, Sayed Ali Albahrani, Dirk Schwantuschke, Peter Brückner, Sandrine Wagner, Rüdiger Quay
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents the design and modeling of two distributed resistive downconverter monolithic microwave integrated circuits (MMICs), realized in 100- and 70-nm AlGaN/GaN high-electron-mobility transistor (HEMT) technologies operating up to D-band frequencies (110–170 GHz). A dedicated transistor model that describes both the 100-nm and the scaled 70-nm HEMTs has been developed and extracted and has been used for the design of high-frequency mixers. The model is based on the physics-based advanced SPICE model for GaN HEMTs (ASM-HEMT). The standard ASM-HEMT model has been adopted by including a more precise model of the influence of velocity saturation and channel-length modulation (CLM) for both drain current and intrinsic capacitances and a cross-coupling (CC) capacitances model, to improve low-voltage mixer simulations up to at least D-band frequencies. The proposed MMICs can be operated either in a drain-pumped or gate-pumped mode. At 9 dBm of local oscillator (LO) power and a fixed intermediate frequency (IF) of 200 MHz, the 70- and 100-nm gate-length drain-pumped MMICs have an average conversion gain (CG) of −8.7 dB and −9.3 dB over a wide radio frequency (RF) band of 50–150 GHz, respectively. In gate-pumped operation, the CG is approximately 2 dB lower. The 100-nm HEMT MMIC has an RF input power related −1-dB compression point (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$P_{\mathrm {-1\text {dB}}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) of 3 dBm in the drain-pumped mode and the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$P_{\mathrm {-1\text {dB}}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> increases to 9 dBm in gate-pumped operation. The 70-nm HEMT MMIC, in comparison, achieves a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$P_{\mathrm {-1\text {dB}}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 2 and 10 dBm for drain and gate LO-driven operations, respectively.

title: Design of Hybrid Battery-Hydrogen Storage Systems for Reliable Renewable Energy Integration and Seasonal Balancing
authors: Holguer Noriega, Riccardo Lazzari, Luigi Piegari
abstract: This paper presents a mathematical framework for the sizing and operation of hybrid battery-hydrogen energy storage systems supporting renewable energy integration with seasonal balancing. The proposed methodology adopts a two-stage approach: in the first stage, a mixed integer linear programming-based energy management system is used to optimize system operation over a full year, accurately capturing seasonal dynamics and operational constraints. In the second stage, component sizing is performed using a discrete particle swarm optimization algorithm with integer constraints, ensuring that all component sizes are technically feasible and compatible with commercially available equipment. The framework explicitly couples operational management and sizing, overcoming the weak integration typical of existing methods, and avoids excessive simplification by maintaining high temporal resolution. Comprehensive sensitivity analyses demonstrate the robustness and adaptability of the optimal hybrid energy storage system configuration under varying techno-economic scenarios. For residential customers, self-consumption increases from 37 % (PV-only) to 47 % with HESS integration, and up to 54 % with self-consumption incentives. A similar trend is observed for industrial applications, where self-consumption rises from 49 % to 61 % with HESS and reaches 67 % with incentives. In both residential and industrial cases, the net present value increases by nearly 30 % when incentives are applied.

title: Design of a 75 kW Three-Level DC/DC GaN Converter for Fuel Cell Electric Powertrains
authors: Simone Giuffrida, Stefano Savio, Fabio Mandrile, Fausto Stella, Eric Giacomo Armando, Radu Bojoi
abstract: Fuel cells are increasingly recognized as a viable alternative to traditional battery packs in electric vehicles, particularly for applications requiring reduced weight and extended range. Despite their advantages, the low and load-dependent output voltage of fuel cell stacks (typically below 200 V) requires high-efficiency boost DC-DC converters to achieve the DC voltage required by the traction inverters, usually in the range 400 V to 800 V. In this context, gallium nitride (GaN) technology enables switching frequencies exceeding 100 kHz, making it a prime candidate for power converters having high power density. This paper presents the design and experimental validation of a 75 kW interleaved multilevel DC–DC boost converter based on GaN power devices, developed to interface fuelcell stacks with 800 V traction inverters. Detailed characterization of the GaN switches is performed via doublepulse testing to properly evaluate the switching losses. This information is used for the thermal design of a liquidcooled heat sink ensuring safe operation below a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$150^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>C junction temperature. Magnetic design includes differential and commonmode inductors finiteelement analysis to meet ripple and powerdensity constraints. The DClink capacitor bank employs PLZT ceramic capacitors to minimize volume and losses. The assembled prototype achieves a volumetric power density of approximately 21 kW/L and a gravimetric density of 15 kW/kg. Dynamic load tests demonstrate a peak efficiency of 98.7<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and robust current sharing and capacitor voltage balancing across all operating conditions.

title: A Multi-Condition Acceleration Slip Regulation for DDEVs Based on Dynamic Torque Allocation
authors: Haoyu Lv, Xiangyu Wang, Zhenhao Luo, Quantong Li, Liang Li
abstract: Distributed drive electric vehicles (DDEVs) enable independent, high bandwidth wheel torque control, but they also introduce slip behaviors that are not well addressed by conventional brake centric acceleration slip regulation (ASR). This paper proposes a fully motor-torque-based ASR framework with dynamic torque allocation (DTA-ASR) to suppress excessive slip across multiple operating conditions without frequent braking, while balancing traction and stability. A reliability aware vehicle speed estimator is developed by assigning jerk based reliability weights to wheel speeds, applying extrema selection during severe slip/braking phases, and enforcing an acceleration consistency constraint to limit transients. Slip targets are scheduled using a Burckhardt μ–λ mapping. A dual layer activation/deactivation logic combines slip ratio and wheel speed difference triggers with secondary verification based on longitudinal dynamics mismatch and motor torque gradient cues. Slip regulation is achieved using adaptive weighting nonlinear model predictive control (AWS-NMPC) formulated in a linear parameter varying (LPV) framework, incorporating a unified hazard index, asymmetric filtering, and rate-limited weight updates. Multi-condition torque coordination is further designed for complex adhesion scenarios, where constrained high-μ torque scaling is applied to mitigate yaw and steering disturbances. Simulations and vehicle experiments against a production multi-loop PID baseline and a non-allocation ASR demonstrate improved slip suppression, reduced oscillation, smoother launches, and mitigated yaw motion.

title: Improvement of Vacuum Thermal Insulation Properties for Ultralightweight Stacked Superconducting Cables
authors: K. Shiohara, M. Kakitani, M. Sato, T. Nakanishi, K. Adachi, T. Izumi, M. Iwakuma
abstract: To enhance efficiency and achieve weight reduction in aircraft electric propulsion systems, the resinification of refrigerant pipes—a critical component of superconducting cables—has garnered considerable attention. This study aims to address the problem of incomplete refrigerant filling evident in resin-corrugated tubes owing to inadequate gas-barrier properties by exploring a novel method to improve these properties. As a surface modification method, plated metal coatings were applied to the resin tubes, and their gas-barrier performance was systematically evaluated. The results demonstrated that the plated resin tubes exhibited excellent gas barrier characteristics, comparable to those of conventional materials, such as SUS304 stainless steel. Additionally, the target weight-to-current ratio of < 1 kg/kA/m—crucial for aircraft applications—was successfully achieved. These findings indicate that the proposed method offers a promising and practical solution for the future implementation of aircraft electric propulsion systems.

title: IEEE Computational Intelligence Society Information
authors: N/A

title: FastCC: A System-Algorithm Co-Design for Connected Components Computation on Large Power-Law Graphs
authors: Menghan Jia, Yongquan Fu, Yiming Zhang, Xinhai Chen, Dongsheng Li
publish: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
abstract: None

title: Improving AMS Verification Efficiency Through Test-Bench and Environment Optimization
authors: Bikram Biswas, Barjeestahseen Mulla, Shrikant Pattar
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: In present System on Chip design, Analog and mixed signal (AMS) circuits play an important role. Due to the complex behaviour of ICs, there are many challenges in verification of overall system. Simulation time for top level AMS verification is one of the biggest challenges in current time due to complex system. In this paper, some of the verification practices have been discussed to improve the simulation speed. Approximately 5 -time improvement of the speed has been observed during our experiment.

title: Edge-AI Enabled IoT Framework for Real-Time Urban Traffic Flow Prediction and Congestion Avoidance
authors: A. Hency Juliet, P. Malathi, B. Geetha, S. Sathish Kumar, Saveetha V
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: To develop an Edge-AI based IoT framework that can accurately predict real-time urban traffic flow and proactively identify congestion using lightweight edge models supported by cloud-based deep learning. Real-time traffic flow prediction plays a critical role in intelligent transportation systems, smart city development, and effective congestion control. Although cloud-based models can deliver high prediction accuracy, they are often constrained by latency, high bandwidth usage, and reliance on uninterrupted network connectivity, which limits their practicality for edge-level deployment. Edge nodes struggle to run complex models due to limited processing power, and variations in IoT sensor data can negatively impact prediction accuracy and congestion identification. To mitigate these challenges, the system employs an LSTM model for high-accuracy cloud prediction alongside a compact Conv1D model optimized for fast, real-time inference on edge devices. The approach delivers precise traffic prediction with the LSTM model and supports rapid, energy-efficient real-time processing on edge devices using the lightweight Conv1D model. The findings indicate that the LSTM model performs well particularly on the PEMS-BAY dataset with an RMSE of 0.6120 and MAE of 0.2082—but its large parameter count limits its suitability for edge devices. In comparison, the lightweight Conv1D model achieves similar accuracy with an RMSE of 0.6167 and MAE of 0.4196 while using only 1,969 parameters, making it far more efficient and fully compatible with TFLite INT8 for real-time edge deployment.

title: Early-Stage Multimodal Lung Cancer Detection Using Attention-Based Feature Fusion
authors: Kokkila Gadda Koteswara Rao, Lalitha Kumari Pappala
abstract: Early-stage lung cancer diagnosis is often hindered by low contrast, subtle tumour morphology, and incomplete feature representation across imaging modalities. To overcome these limitations, we present the Multi-Stage Cross-Element Attention Quantum Convolutional Transformer with Group Aggregation Network (MSEGA-Net), an advanced multitask framework designed for segmentation and classification of multimodal lung cancer images. MSEGA-Net incorporates PNSTG-UNet++ for adaptive contrast enhancement and artefact suppression, Dual-Path Vision Transformers to capture comprehensive global–local dependencies, and a quantum-inspired convolutional layer for efficient high-dimensional feature encoding. The cross-element attention module facilitates fine-grained inter-modal fusion, while group aggregation enhances semantic consistency and long-range contextual reasoning essential for accurate early lesion identification. Extensive evaluations conducted on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that MSEGA-Net achieves existing performance with average classification accuracies ranging from 99.20% to 99.85%, as reported in the corresponding result tables, with statistical significance confirmed using ANOVA and Friedman tests (p ≤ 0.001). Furthermore, model interpretability is enhanced through Grad-CAM++–based visual explanations and LLM-assisted clinical reasoning, supporting transparent and trustworthy clinical decision support.Overall, MSEGA-Net offers a robust, generalizable, and explainable solution for early lung cancer detection, showcasing significant potential for real-world clinical integration.

title: Residual Value Prediction in Automotive: A Review of Methods and Data with Research Roadmap
authors: Fatima Rabia Yapicioglu, Alberto Rigenti, Andrea Cisci, Meltem Aksoy, Fabio Vitali, Luca Longo
abstract: Residual value prediction (RVP) has become a cornerstone of automotive economics, shaping leasing, resale, insurance, fleet management, and long-term brand positioning. Inaccurate predictions can lead to severe financial and strategic consequences: undervaluation undermines competitiveness, while overvaluation erodes profitability and trust. Artificial intelligence (AI) has opened new avenues for enhancing predictive accuracy, giving rise to diverse approaches that leverage heterogeneous data sources, including vehicle condition, ownership history, and macroeconomic factors, alongside advanced modelling techniques and varying levels of transparency and reliability. Given this diversity, a comprehensive synthesis of the field is needed to evaluate current practices and outline future research directions that integrate methodological rigour, multidimensional data inclusion, and principles of trustworthy RVP. This study offers a comprehensive synthesis of the literature by organising existing work into four themes: (i) data-related factors, (ii) prediction and modelling methodologies, (iii) evaluation approaches, and (iv) mechanisms for transparency and trustworthiness. Our proposed research roadmap is guided by responsible AI principles, emphasising the importance of explanations that are both interpretable and reliable for RVP. By consolidating prior contributions and identifying open research opportunities, this study seeks to advance the development of RVP systems that are accurate, transparent, and reliable, thereby supporting more trustworthy decision-making across the automotive ecosystem.

title: System Frequency Nadir and Trajectory Prediction with Discontinuity Constraints in Governor Dynamics
authors: Jongoh Baek, Luke Lowery, Adam B. Birchfield
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: Accurately predicting the frequency nadir and estimating the overall frequency trajectory are crucial analytical tasks in power system planning. Given the large number of operating scenarios and contingency events that must be evaluated, low-order frequency nadir prediction (FNP) models have been recently developed to avoid the computational burden of full dynamic simulations in large, complex systems. However, a major technical limitation of existing FNP models is their inability to capture inherent discontinuities such as limits, piecewise functions, and deadbands that strongly influence the actual frequency dynamics. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a discontinuity-aware frequency nadir prediction (DA-FNP) model that explicitly implements discontinuity constraints into the frequency response estimation. By implementing these discontinuities, the model not only predicts the system frequency trajectory with high fidelity but also identifies which generators are subject to enforced constraints. This capability provides new insights for system planners, enabling a more realistic evaluation of frequency security margins and resource adequacy in future power systems with high renewable penetration. The methodology is validated against detailed dynamic simulations on both small-and large-scale synthetic grids. The case study demonstrates significant enhancement on the accuracy of system configuration and system frequency trajectory, while retaining computational efficiency of low-order models. Furthermore, the approach offers a practical and scalable tool for planning studies in large, complex power systems.

title: RIS-Aided RSMA Improves the Latency Vs. Energy Trade-Off in the Finite Block Length MIMO Downlink
authors: Mohammad Soleymani, Bruno Clerckx, Robert Schober, Lajos Hanzo
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: We simultaneously minimize the latency and improve energy efficiency (EE) of the multi-user multiple-input multiple-output (MU-MIMO) rate splitting multiple access (RSMA) downlink, aided by a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS). Our results show that RSMA improves the EE and may reduce the delay to 13% of that of spatial division multiple access (SDMA). Moreover, RIS and RSMA support each other synergistically, while an RIS operating without RSMA provides limited benefits in terms of latency and cannot effectively mitigate interference. Furthermore, increasing the RIS size amplifies the gains of RSMA more significantly than those of SDMA, without altering the fundamental EE-latency trade-offs. Results also show that latency increases with more stringent reliability requirements, and RSMA yields more significant gains under such conditions, making it eminently suitable for energy-efficient ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) scenarios.

title: Comparision Between 8 Bit ALU Using CLA & RCA
authors: Sarbatriki Jana, Soumyarup Dutta, Raina Das, Meesha Sinha, Mili Sarkar
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Arithmetic Logic Units are basic building blocks of modern digital systems, having considerable impacts on the overall processor performance, power consumption, and silicon area. Among several adder architectures used within ALU designs, the Ripple Carry Adder and Carry Look-Ahead Adder are the most used owing to their simplicity and performance advantages, respectively. This work proposes a detailed design and implementation of an 8-bit ALU using the RCA and CLA architectures. The proposed designs provide both arithmetic and logical operations and have been implemented using Verilog HDL. Functional verification and synthesis have been done using Xilinx Vivado. A comparative analysis is performed based on performance (delay), power consumption, and area utilization. Simulation results depict that while the RCA-based ALU offers lower area and simpler design, the CLA-based ALU significantly outperforms the RCA in terms of speed, suitable for high-performance applications.

title: Dynamic Browser Threat Detection Leveraging Multi-Engine Threat Analytics
authors: Mehul Kumar Das, Varun Shukla, Abhay Soni, Vivek Shukla, Rishabh Tiwari, Priya Pal
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The ubiquitous spread of malware through downloadable files is a major security threat to end-users. Although antivirus products provide reactive protection, what is needed is proactive protection where threats are identified before a file is even downloaded. In this paper, a lightweight real-time Chrome extension is introduced that scans downloadable links on a webpage through the VirusTotal API. The extension uses a whitelisting process and VirusTotal’s cloud antivirus scanning service in the cloud to mark malicious files ahead of the start of the download process. Experimental evaluation shows the efficacy of the proposed method in sensing malicious files before their download, thus increasing protection for users and encouraging safer web practices.

title: Integrating Quantum Generative Models with Deep Learning for Skin Disease Diagnosis and Data Augmentation
authors: K. Sudharson, V.E.N.V. Mani Pavan, Pothuru Likhitha
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Constructing high-quality medical images for analysis in dermatology is persistently challenging due to the lack of annotation datasets which hinder the accuracy of the analytic assessment. This research utilizes Quantum Generative Adversarial Network (QGAN) in combination with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with attention mechanisms to improve image quality via data augmentation. Low datasets with low resolutions such as HAM10000 and Derm7pt showed much higher results with an SSIM of 0.96 and a PSNR of 31.8 dB which is an astonishing increase in image fidelity from standard augmentation with GANs. The QGAN model also showed much lower noise and standard deviation values of $\mathbf{8. 7 \%}$ and $\mathbf{1 1. 2 \%}$ on the test sets. This framework also boosted up diagnostic performance on unusual types of skin diseases which are available on the dataset in small amounts. This research paves a way for the application of quantum powered AI in dermatology for effective real-time remote dermatology and advanced medical systems.
