title: Material Evaluation of Permanent Magnet Assisted Synchronous Reluctance Motor for High Torque Density Electric Vehicle Drives
authors: B.V. Sai Thrinath, Yerasi Mahitha, Sake Prabhakar, Thirumala Karthivya, Pamu Sudharsansairam, V. Bhargavi Boyinasetty
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The paper explores the torque comparison of a 96 V, $5.5 \mathrm{~A}, 0.4 \mathrm{~kW}$ permanent magnet assisted synchronous reluctance motor with a $\mathbf{7 5 0 ~ r p m}$. It analyzes the composite effect of ceramic and NdFeB permanent magnet material together with various silicon steel laminations of 64F320, M36-29GA, M340-50E and M1000-100. The electromagnetic simulation is performed under the same operating conditions to compare the maximum torque, minimum torque, average torque and the torque ripple. The findings indicate that the performance of permanent magnets in terms of torque is strongly dependent on the selected permanent magnet material and lamination grade. The results provides the optimal choice of the material in design of permanent magnet assisted synchronous reluctance motors that can be used in electric driving applications.

title: Hazard Hunter: An Intelligent Rover for Risk Detection
authors: P. Ramchandar Rao, Ch.Rajendra Prasad
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The increasing risks of fire hazards, toxic gas leaks, and environmental obstacles necessitate intelligent autonomous safety systems. This study presents the Intelligent Rover, an AI-powered robotic platform with obstacle avoidance and gas and fire detection capabilities. For real-time autonomous danger mitigation, it incorporates multi- sensor fusion employing flame sensors, MQ gas sensors, and ultrasonic sensors. The rover carries out adaptive course planning and risk assessment with the help of an integrated microcontroller and AI-based decision-making. Instant notifications and remote monitoring are made possible via a wireless module. The rover performs well in hazardous and industrial settings thanks to deep learning and sensor navigation. Comparing testing to current safety robots reveal superior risk management, faster reaction times, and enhanced danger recognition, by providing real-time hazard warnings, adaptive navigation, and scalable deployment, the Intelligent Rover raises the bar for security and safety.

title: Multi-Window Timeframe Temporal Features for Stroke-Risk Prediction
authors: Chanon Thansawd, Sittisak Saechueng, Peerasak Painprasit, Eri Sato-Shimokawara, Yosuke Fukuchi, Nobuyuki Nishiuchi, Suwanna Rasmequan, Waranrach Viriyavit
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Stroke is a major global health burden. The early stroke risk prediction is essential for reducing disability and mortality. Most existing machine-learning approaches rely on single-shot clinical records, which fail to capture the longitudinal health dynamics that precede stroke onset. To address this limitation, this study constructs temporal representations across three retrospective observation windows. Statistical, temporal, and cross-window features were generated to capture both short-term variability and long-term physiological trends, and three hierarchical feature sets were designed to evaluate the impact of increasing featureengineering complexity. ANOVA F-tests and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) were used for dimensionality reduction. Model performance was subsequently evaluated using Logistic Regression with Leave-One-Out CrossValidation (LOOCV) under conditions of severe class imbalance. The results showed that the temporal model outperformed the single-shot baseline with a maximum GMean of 0.638. McNemar's test further confirmed that temporal representations yielded statistically significant improvements and indicated minimal differences between PCA and ANOVA in prediction behavior. Overall, the results indicate that incorporating temporal information substantially enhances early stroke-risk prediction, providing more reliable decision-support capability than conventional single-time-point approaches.

title: A Hierarchical Clustering Based Evolutionary Algorithm for Multimodal Multiobjective Optimization
authors: Ying Huang, Xianjian Cao, Zhou Yang
publish: 2025 21st International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Security (CIS)
abstract: Multi-modal multi-objective optimization tasks (MMOPs) involve locating several distinct yet equivalent Pareto solution sets (PSs) within the decision space, all of which correspond to an identical Pareto front (PF) in the objective space. However, existing multi-modal multi-objective evolutionary algorithms (MMEAs) often suffer from imbalanced solution distributions or fail to preserve high-quality local PSs due to dominance pressure and inadequate diversity maintenance. To address these issues, we propose HRSCD, a novel MMEA that integrates hierarchical ranking with a specialized crowding distance mechanism. The crowding distance is enhanced by Affinity Propagation (AP) clustering within each non-dominated front, enabling more accurate and adaptive diversity estimation. Meanwhile, hierarchical ranking incorporates a tunable parameter to selectively archive individuals that are non-dominated with respect to the global PF, thereby preserving both convergence and local solution diversity. Experimental evaluations on 22 benchmark instances demonstrate that HRSCD significantly outperforms six state-of-the-art MMEAs in terms of multi-modal performance metrics. The results confirm its effectiveness in maintaining well-distributed, high-quality equivalent PSs, making it a competitive approach for solving complex MMOPs.

title: Diagnosing Heteroskedasticity and Resolving Multicollinearity Paradoxes in Physicochemical Property Prediction
authors: Malikussaid, Septian Caesar Floresko, Ade Romadhony, Isman Kurniawan, Warih Maharani, Hilal Hudan Nuha
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Lipophilicity (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\log \mathrm{P}$</tex>) prediction remains central to drug discovery, yet linear regression models for this task frequently violate statistical assumptions in ways that invalidate their reported performance metrics. We analyzed 426,850 bioactive molecules from a rigorously curated intersection of PubChem, ChEMBL, and eMolecules databases, revealing severe heteroskedasticity in linear models predicting computed logP values (XLOGP3): residual variance increases 4.2-fold in lipophilic regions (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\log \mathrm{P}$</tex> greater than 5) compared to balanced regions (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\log P 2$</tex> to 4). Classical remediation strategiesWeighted Least Squares and Box-Cox transformation-failed to resolve this violation (Breusch-Pagan p-value less than 0.0001 for all variants). Tree-based ensemble methods (Random Forest <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}$</tex>-squared of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0. 7 6 4$</tex>, XGBoost <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}$</tex>-squared of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0. 7 6 5$</tex>) proved inherently robust to heteroskedasticity while delivering superior predictive performance. SHAP analysis resolved a critical multicollinearity paradox: despite a weak bivariate correlation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0. 1 4 6$</tex>, molecular weight emerged as the single most important predictor (mean absolute SHAP value of 0.573), with its effect suppressed in simple correlations by confounding with topological polar surface area (TPSA). These findings demonstrate that standard linear models face fundamental challenges for computed lipophilicity prediction and provide a principled framework for interpreting ensemble models in QSAR applications.

title: Sensor-Driven Dialogue System for Collecting Community-Specific Knowledge Data
authors: Naoki Tobase, Eri Sato-Shimokawara
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: To enable context-aware support in human-robot interaction, this study proposes a sensor-driven interactive system that synchronizes with real-world events to capture users' behaviors and mental states. Recent advances in integrating large language models (LLMs) with robotics have improved perception and control capabilities. These systems are built on LLMs, which possess internet-scale knowledge but often lack understanding of user-specific behaviors and mental states in daily life. Addressing this gap, we developed a system that triggers user queries based on sensor events-such as motion detection or sound localization-and collects answer data on current, preceding, and subsequent behaviors, as well as mental states before and after the current behaviors. In a laboratory setting involving 19 participants, we confirmed that both the sensor-driven and time-driven methods successfully collected community-specific knowledge. Comparing the two approaches further suggests that the sensor-driven system may achieve a higher response rate with lower user burden, while also tending to capture a more diverse set of current behaviors-although these trends require validation with larger, longer-term datasets. As a step toward enabling robots to better understand user behavior in real-world environments, this study demonstrates the potential of sensor-driven interaction as a foundation for accumulating community-specific knowledge. Future work will explore longterm deployment, analyses using higher-performance embedding models, and structuring the collected data into a knowledge representation suitable for integration with LLMs.

title: Role of AI in Blockchain Scalability
authors: Musthafa Kamal Pasha A, A. Hency Juliet
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Blockchain scalability remains a central constraint on the deployment of decentralized applications at industrial scale, as throughput, latency, and resource costs are jointly limited by consensus overhead, network dynamics, and adversarial conditions. Recent advances in artificial intelligence provide a complementary optimization layer for blockchain systems by enabling data driven control of protocol parameters, adaptive consensus behavior, workload aware sharding, and intelligent Layer 2 coordination. This paper examines the role of artificial intelligence in improving blockchain scalability across the system stack, including predictive and reinforcement learning based tuning of block intervals, block sizes, validator scheduling, and difficulty adjustment, artificial intelligence assisted sharding strategies that reduce cross shard communication and rebalance committee formation under heterogeneous load, intelligent transaction and mempool management that mitigates congestion and improves confirmation time predictability, and artificial intelligence supported Layer 2 and cross chain mechanisms that reduce on chain data footprints while maintaining correctness guarantees. The paper synthesizes contemporary research into a unified taxonomy that maps artificial intelligence methods such as supervised learning, deep reinforcement learning, graph learning, Bayesian optimization, and ensemble approaches to scalability objectives and measurable performance indicators including throughput, end to end latency, finality, communication complexity, and energy per transaction. The analysis highlights emerging design patterns such as closed loop protocol control, robust learning under adversarial environments, and cost aware optimization across the scalability trilemma, while identifying unresolved challenges including safe exploration, verifiability of learned decisions, distribution shift, and governance of adaptive protocols. The paper concludes by outlining a research agenda for trustworthy artificial intelligence enabled scalability, emphasizing reproducible benchmarking, formal constraints for learning driven control, and interoperable architectures that preserve decentralization while achieving elastic performance.

title: Spatial-Frequency Contrastive Learning for Self-Supervised Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images
authors: Yujia Fu, Mingyang Wang, Danfeng Hong, Gemine Vivone
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Self-supervised learning has shown strong potential for remote sensing semantic segmentation by exploiting large amounts of unlabeled data. However, existing methods face several challenges when applied to remote sensing images, including limited use of frequency-domain information, inconsistent semantics caused by conventional view generation, and suboptimal uniform masking strategies for dense scenes. To address these issues, we propose Spatial-Frequency Contrastive Learning (SFCL), a dual-domain self-supervised framework that jointly models spatial and frequency representations. SFCL enforces spatial-domain consistency to capture local structural information, frequency-domain consistency to encode global structural and spectral patterns, and cross-domain alignment to enhance semantic invariance. In addition, we introduce a Routing-Based Hybrid Masking Strategy (RB-HMS), which adaptively routes selected patches to pixel-level or patch-level masking branches, balancing fine-grained object preservation and contextual reasoning. Extensive experiments on multiple remote sensing datasets demonstrate that SFCL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art self-supervised methods on semantic segmentation tasks, validating its effectiveness in learning robust and semantically meaningful representations for remote sensing applications.

title: Detecting Coastal Aggregation of Macro-Plastic Litter Using Satellite X-Band SAR Imagery
authors: Ferdinando Nunziata, Nicola Angelo Famiglietti, Anna Verlanti, Andrea Buono, Antonino Memmolo, Luigi di Michele, Annamaria Vicari, Alessandro Fanti, Maurizio Migliaccio
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: This study is to summarize the lesson learned during the July 2024 experimental campaign where targets, consisting of floating plastic and natural debris, were jointly observed in a South Italy lake by drone and satellite instruments. The satellite layer consists of the X-band Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) equipped onboard the Italian Cosmo-SkyMed Second Generation (CSG) constellation, while the drone layer consists of an optical camera. A set of multi-polarization features, namely the co- and cross-polarized normalized radar cross section (NRCS), the SPAN and the like-to-cross-polarization ratio, are extracted from the CSG SAR measurements. Their sensitivity to the plastic target, along with their ability to discriminate plastic from natural floating debris, are investigated using objective metrics. Experimental results, obtained under calm water conditions, show that floating plastic calls for co- and cross-polarized signals well-above the system noise and well-distinguishable from the surrounding water using all the features. The plastic target appears as a spot brighter than the surrounding water in both the co- and the cross-polarized NRCS imagery. The co-polarized NRCS calls for the largest plastic-to-water contrast and the largest separation between the plastic and the natural target. However, this separation is such that the discrimination is a challenging task when using intensity-based dual-polarimetric features.

title: YOLOVisionSafe for Real-Time Industrial Safety Monitoring with Object Detection and Pose Estimation
authors: Prashanna R, Dasari Vishal, Aravind Krishna Sv, Midhru Jayan J, Lalithamani N
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Industrial accidents continue to be a cause of significant concern for the entire world even after stringent safety regulations. Factory environments commonly experience problems such as absence of Personal Protective Equipment and worker posture hazards, which result in injuries, deaths, and loss of productivity. Manual monitoring is time-intensive, prone to errors, and does not provide real-time feedback. This paper presents an AI-powered real-time vision system for monitoring the safety of workers using advanced deep learning methods. It incorporates strong worker detection, Personal Protective Equipment detection (helmets, vests, gloves, boots, and goggles), and human pose estimation. The model uses RT-DETR for worker detection, YOLOv8 for PPE detection, and ViTPose for precise pose estimation so that it gives strong performance in challenging industrial environments. Experimental testing establishes precision of 0.81 and recall of 0.71 for Personal Protective Equipment detection. Pose estimation establishes a Percentage of Correct Keypoints of 0.87 and Mean Per Joint Position Error of 6.8 px, reflecting high precision in posture analysis. In comparison with baseline systems, our system exhibits significant improvement in detection robustness and responsiveness, which feeds back into scalable and proactive industrial safety solutions.

title: Building Energy and ESS Charging Discharging Prediction: Performance Comparison of LSTM and Transformer Based on Structurally Different Input Data
authors: Heehoon Woo, Jounghun Kim, Haneul Shin, Xianda Liu, Guwon Yoon, Tacklim Lee, Seulah Park, Sehyun Park
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Power and Renewable Energy Engineering (PREE)
abstract: This study fairly compares Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Transformer models by enforcing a single shared pipeline with identical physical constraints-state-of-charge (SOC) bounds, charge/discharge efficiencies, and rated power limits. We evaluate using mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) under two cases. In Case 1, models train on a mixed dataset combining a 2020 photovoltaic (PV) plant with January-July 2023 building data and are tested on August 2023. In Case 2, training uses only January-July 2023 building data and testing is again August 2023. All preprocessing (normalization, imputation, and Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA) weather matching) is fit exclusively on the training split, and group-aware chronological splits by time and site prevent leakage. We further assess regime robustness by decomposing results into clear-day and overcast-day subsets. For visualization, we focus on August 2023 calendar days that were pre-flagged as adverse by KMA forecasts using temperature, humidity, wind speed, and precipitation; these days are ranked into severity tiers, and only this prioritized subset is plotted. To analyze operational implications, we derive a proxy ESS charge/discharge signal from the power-balance equation and examine the resulting charge/discharge and grid-import profiles, which provide qualitative evidence of peak-spike mitigation and PV-aligned charging. Value-based metrics (cost, peak reduction, self-consumption) are deferred to future work.

title: Synthesis of Double-Mode SAW Filters: Bridging Reactance Models and Physical Structures for Advanced RF Applications
authors: Ángel Romero, Ricardo Pampliega, Ayon Kumar Das, Rafaat Mansour, Jordi Verdú, Pedro De Paco
abstract: This work presents a synthesis methodology for Double-Mode Surface Acoustic Wave (DMS) filters linking reactance-level synthesis with the physical behavior of the structure. By combining the Coupling-of-Modes (COM) model with trisection and extracted-pole topologies, the method provides a direct mapping from coupling matrix (CM) parameters to geometric variables such as the IDT gap L<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</sub>, grating pitch P<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</sub>, and aperture W. A key contribution of this work is the explicit connection established between the synthesis formulation and the physical geometry of the DMS structure, enabling a clear interpretation of how geometric parameters—particularly the IDT gap and aperture—govern the resulting filter performance. The methodology further enables the incorporation of gradient-based optimization tools, yielding reproducible and robust designs, and supports fine-tuning for manufacturability to ensure practical layouts while preserving the desired transmission response. Validation is demonstrated through simulations and experimental measurements for a 605 MHz stand-alone filter fabricated on 42°Y-cut LiTaO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>, where synthesized, COM-simulated, and measured responses show good agreement for the baseline implementation. The approach enables reproducible and physically interpretable DMS designs adaptable to a wide range of bandwidth requirements.

title: A Deep Learning Framework of Multi-Class Diabetic Retinopathy Detection with an Explainable Attention
authors: Manjeet Kour, Kapil Saini
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Preventable vision loss (Diabetic retinopathy) is one of the major problems endangering the vision of the people, and this necessitates accurate and reliable automated diagnostic systems. This paper introduces a framework of attention-guided convolutional neural network that is used to classify multi-class diabetic retinopathy based on retinal fundus images of a clinically validated dataset. The given methodology is a combination of convolutional feature extractor with an attention mechanism that will improve the localization of pathological regions and the discriminative power between the disease severity levels. Extensive assessment based on quantitative and qualitative data reveal a classification accuracy of 99.8, precision, recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score of all classes are above 99.5%. The discriminative capability of the framework is also very high with near-perfect area-under-curve values. The analysis of visual attention also proves that the model pays attention to the clinically significant retinal structures and enhances transparency and confidence in the automated decision-making. The comparison of the proposed method with the current one demonstrates the advantages of the new methodology, which makes it effective in retinopathy screening of diabetics with high accuracy and rendering the results explainable.

title: Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Advancements in Computational Sciences (ICACS)

title: MedXplain-VQA: Multi-Component Explainable Medical Visual Question Answering
authors: Hai-Dang Nguyen, Minh-Anh Dang, Minh-Tan Le, Minh-Tuan Le
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
abstract: Explainability is critical for the clinical adoption of medical visual question answering (VQA) systems, as physicians require transparent reasoning to trust AI-generated diagnoses. We present MedXplain-VQA, a comprehensive framework integrating five explainable AI components to deliver interpretable medical image analysis. The framework leverages a finetuned BLIP-2 backbone, medical query reformulation, enhanced Grad-CAM attention, precise region extraction, and structured chain-of-thought reasoning via multi-modal language models. To evaluate the system, we introduce a medical-domain-specific framework replacing traditional NLP metrics with clinically relevant assessments, including terminology coverage, clinical structure quality, and attention region relevance. Experiments on 500 PathVQA histopathology samples demonstrate substantial improvements, with the enhanced system achieving a composite score of 0.683 compared to 0.378 for baseline methods, while maintaining high reasoning confidence (0.890). Our system identifies 3-5 diagnostically relevant regions per sample and generates structured explanations averaging 57 words with appropriate clinical terminology. Ablation studies reveal that query reformulation provides the most significant initial improvement, while chain-of-thought reasoning enables systematic diagnostic processes. These findings underscore the potential of MedXplainVQA as a robust, explainable medical VQA system. Future work will focus on validation with medical experts and large-scale clinical datasets to ensure clinical readiness.

title: Synergistic Evaluation of Vortex Generator Layout Optimization and Fatigue Life for UAV Wings Based on the ANSYS Platform
authors: Liwen Deng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Aerospace Engineering (ICMTAE)
abstract: To address the issue in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) wing design where aerodynamic performance optimization and structural fatigue life assessment are disconnected, leading to insufficient design reliability or low efficiency, this paper proposes a synergistic evaluation and optimization method based on the ANSYS platform. This method establishes a parametric fluid-structure interaction model, comprehensively utilizing Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), Finite Element Analysis (FEA), and linear cumulative damage theory. Combined with Response Surface Methodology (RSM) and a Multi-Objective Genetic Algorithm (MOGA), it optimizes the layout parameters of vortex generators (VGs) to achieve simultaneous enhancement of the wing's lift-to-drag ratio and fatigue life. Through numerical experiments and comparative analysis, it is concluded that, compared to a design solely pursuing aerodynamic optimality, the proposed synergistic optimization method can ensure that the local stress at the VG root meets durability requirements while achieving an acceptable aerodynamic performance gain (an 8.5% increase in lift-to-drag ratio). This ultimately yields a globally optimal layout scheme that satisfies the design life requirement (greater than 105 cycles), effectively resolving the conflict between multidisciplinary performance metrics.

title: DDGN: Dual-Channel Dual-Mode Graph Networks for Node Classification under Noisy Topology
authors: Guanxian Li, Fasheng Zhou, Wensheng Zhang, Xuebing Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) exploit topological structures—namely, node-to-node connections—to aggregate contextual information, thereby achieving strong performance across diverse domains. Representative models such as Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) and Graph Attention Networks (GAT) have demonstrated remarkable success in node-level tasks. Nevertheless, two fundamental issues remain: (1) inaccurate topologies directly misguide the neighborhood aggregation process, degrading classification accuracy; (2) the additional structural information introduces redundant or noisy signals, further impairing generalization. To address these issues, we propose a Dual-channel Dual-mode Graph Network (DDGN) that reconstructs a feature graph driven by node-feature similarity. DDGN employs two parallel channels to extract representations from the original topology and the similarity-induced graph, respectively; each channel integrates two complementary feature-extraction schemes and an adaptive switch that dynamically harmonizes their interaction within the channel for richer representation. A lightweight feature-enhancement module and an adaptive weight allocation mechanism are further introduced to synergistically fuse the dual representations. Extensive experiments on multiple public benchmarks show that DDGN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art GNNs in node classification accuracy.

title: Bidirectional Effects of the Remote-sensing Reflectance in Coastal Turbid Waters
authors: Shiming Lu, Shuangyan He, Yanzhen Gu, Peiliang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote-sensing reflectance (Rrs ) is the first-level product of atmospheric correction in ocean color remote sensing, and it directly affects the accuracy of subsequent water color inversion products. The bidirectional effects of Rrs have been primarily explored in phytoplankton-dominated seawater, while limited efforts have been made to explore turbid water rich in sediments. In this paper, based on the Rrs simulated using Hydrolight with the solar zenith angle (θs) in a range of 0°-75°, viewing zenith angle ( θv) from 0° to 87.5°, relative azimuth angle (φ) from 0° to 180°, wavelength in 400-900 nm, the bidirectional effects of Rrs in varying optical water types from clear to sediments-dominated turbid water were quantified. Generally, the greater bidirectional effects tend to occur at larger sun-viewing angles, clearer water and shorter wavelengths. Of various factors affecting bidirectional reflectance effect, the angles have the greater influence, among which, the θv has the largest mean relative difference of 13%±11%, followed by the φ (12%±4%) and θs (11%±3%). The wavelength and water types account for a mean relative difference of 11%±1%. In turbid water, bidirectional effects at red and near-infrared bands are also necessary to be considered. Furthermore, two commonly used bidirectional correction methods of MAG2002 and Lee2011 were applied and examined in this study. The Lee2011 generally outperforms MAG2002 from clear to turbid water. However, the Lee2011 tends to deteriorate at large solar-viewing angles and long wavelengths in turbid water with high-load sediments. This study contributes to understand the comparability of remotely sensed data measured under varying solar-viewing geometries and optical water types, and can help in further reducing the uncertainty of bidirectional effects in coastal water color remote sensing.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: HAP-ANN: Hierarchical Authentication Privacy-Enhanced Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search for Digital Healthcare
authors: Sijing Chen, Guangzhao Bai, Rong Sun, Lei Liu, Jingwei Liu, Jiawen Kang, Shahid Mumtaz, Mianxiong Dong
abstract: With the rapid digital transformation of the healthcare industry, large-scale patient data are increasingly analyzed to support intelligent medical services, where similarity-based retrieval over high-dimensional medical datasets has become a fundamental operation for identifying comparable cases and assisting diagnosis. However, conventional nearest neighbor search mechanisms expose sensitive queries to the database and require exhaustive similarity computation, leading to potential privacy risks and substantial computational overhead. Furthermore, many existing privacy-preserving retrieval frameworks do not adequately integrate access control mechanisms, making it difficult to regulate user permissions in multi-source medical data sharing environments. To address these challenges, we propose HAP-ANN, a hierarchical authentication and privacy-enhanced approximate nearest neighbor search scheme. HAP-ANN employs locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to enable efficient approximate similarity search while preserving essential similarity characteristics. In addition, distributed point functions (DPF) are integrated within a two-server architecture to support privacy-preserving query evaluation and enforce fine-grained access control over the authorized search scope. Theoretical analysis and experimental results demonstrate that HAP-ANN achieves secure, efficient, and low-overhead nearest neighbor retrieval in privacy-sensitive medical environments.

title: Social-Aware Integration of Network Connection, Control, and Service for Computing Resource Management
authors: Ailing Zhong, Dapeng Wu, Ruyan Wang
abstract: With the proliferation of artificial intelligence applications, computing resources indicate a trend of ubiquitous deployment. However, traditional network architectures struggle to efficiently leverage computing resources for personalized request guarantees. Considering the unevenness and heterogeneity of computing resources, as well as the diversity of tasks in computing power networks (CPNs), SI-CRM, an social-aware integration of network connection, control, and service for computing resource management scheme is proposed. Based on social network analysis theory, this scheme models CPNs and comprehensively extracts endogenous social features from multiple dimensions. Then, propagation dynamics theory is utilized to predict the network cascade structure with the help of real-time sensing of node connectivity status, resource availability, and service requirements. Furthermore, endogenous social features are exploited to encourage resource node collaboration, and experience replay is introduced into deep reinforcement learning, thereby promoting proactive collaboration between connection and control in the network southbound. Finally, deep integration between control and service in the network northbound is realized by leveraging resource pre-scheduling and instance sharing. Simulation results verify the superiority of the proposed resource management scheme in reducing the overhead of CPNs.

title: Radio Radiance Field: The New Frontier of Spatial Wireless Channel Representation
authors: Haijian Sun, Feng Ye
abstract: Massive MIMO, among other ground-breaking technologies, is developed for the next-generation wireless systems to support requirements in terms of data rates, reliability, latency, intelligence, security and energy efficiency. Accurate channel estimation remains a key challenge in fully exploiting massive MIMO. While recent research has explored aspects such as near-field effects, spatial non-stationarity, and channel sparsity, many practical estimation and modeling techniques still provide limited channel state information (CSI), often dominated by aggregate channel gain and delay, without full spatial characteristics. Although wideband models and phased-array techniques can capture delay and angular information, many practical estimation methods still lack comprehensive spatial resolution, including polarization, which limits their effectiveness for advanced massive MIMO techniques. This article introduces the concept of radio radiance field (RRF), which captures the spatial distribution and directionality of radio propagation. From RRF, a comprehensive spatial representation of the wireless channel, referred to as Spatial-CSI, can be derived. Owing to the comprehensive geometric and radio information, RRF can be implemented directly for beamforming, delay-alignment modulation, and many other techniques in massive MIMO and reflective intelligent surface implementations. An RRF can also serve as a digital radio twin, which is a virtual representation of the radio environment that includes both geometric structure and radio propagation characteristics, enabling real-time simulation and optimization of wireless systems. It paves the way for various applications from communications to sensing in future networks.

title: Optimization of Antibody Candidates to Mutated Antigens via a Paratope-Informed Pipeline
authors: Fan Xu, Yinglan Feng, Chengyu Yang, Zhi-An Huang, Kay Chen Tan
publish: 2025 21st International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Security (CIS)
abstract: Antibody optimization is critical for improving binding affinity and stability against mutated antigens. However, state-of-the-art methods often overlook the binding mode between the antibody and the original antigen, limiting their effectiveness. Thus, we integrate the analysis of binding mode into the antibody optimization process. To be specific, we propose a computational pipeline that integrates paratope identification, antigen alignment, and residue-level optimization, leveraging a diffusion-based generative model (DiffAb). We validated the pipeline through experiments on antibody restoration and optimization. Key evaluation metrics, including Amino Acid Recovery Rate (AAR), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{C}_{\alpha}$</tex> Root-Mean-Square Deviation (RMSD), Epitope Hit Ratio (EHR), and Improvement in Curvage Error Change (ICEC), guided the selection of candidates for molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. In antibody restoration experiment, our pipeline demonstrated competitive performance against baselines on CVB1-related antibody-antigen complexes. For antibody optimization, we successfully refined CVB5-binding antibodies to interact effectively with CVB1 and CVB3 antigens. MD simulation results further confirmed the stability of the optimized antibodies in binding mutated antigens. These findings highlight the pipeline's capability to adapt antibodies to mutated antigens, providing a robust and versatile tool for therapeutic antibody design.

title: AI-Driven Clinical Decision Support System Using EHR for Heart Failure
authors: Shaik Irfan, Lopa Mandal, Palle Varshith Reddy, Gajjala. Sri Vardhan Reddy, Shaik Sameer Basha
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: This work tells the design and development of an AI based decision support system for health care fields using electronic health record (EHR) data for heart failure. The conceive platform employs ML and natural language processing to process structured and unstructured clinical information, such as, laboratory results, and patient history, to support physicians in diagnosis, treatment planning, and timely recognition of high-risk patients. A user-centered prototype is constructed with actual world datasets and illustrates how interpretable predictions and data insights can enhance outcomes of care, mitigate diagnostic mistakes, and assist data-driven proactive decision-making in medical care for Heart Failure. The present work used Random Forest and Logistic Regression for the prediction. Random forest outperformed Logistic Regression by achieving 88.2% recall, which is a most vital metric in clinical decisions.

title: Real-Time AI-Generated Face Detection: A Deep Learning Framework using GAN
authors: P. Ravi Sankar, Prakya Muppavarapu, Devada Pavan Kumar, Guduru Harshit
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), AI-generated faces have become nearly indistinguishable from real ones, posing severe challenges in verifying image authenticity. This paper proposes a deep learning-based detection framework that leverages a GAN discriminator to classify real and synthetic faces. A balanced dataset was constructed using 10,000 real images from the Flickr-Faces-HQ (FFHQ) dataset and 10,000 synthetic images generated via Style-GAN2 from thispersondoesnotexist.com. The model was trained and evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC metrics. Benchmark comparisons with ResNet-50 and XceptionNet demonstrate that our proposed model achieves state-of-the-art performance with 98.58% accuracy and an AUC of 0.999. A lightweight Flask-based web interface enables real-time classification, contributing to digital forensics and misinformation prevention.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Epsilon Constraint Handling in Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization
authors: Kejian Pang, Zhiyuan Cai, Qiqi Xie, Yanglin Chen, Haoyu Luo, Chaoda Peng
publish: 2025 21st International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Security (CIS)
abstract: Balancing constraint compliance with objective optimization represents a substantial challenge in constrained multiobjective optimization problems (CMOPs). Most constrainthandling methods rely on fixed strategies that struggle to adapt to the changing preferences for feasibility and optimality at different evolutionary stages. This work introduces an adaptive epsilonconstraint method that integrates deep reinforcement learning with evolutionary algorithms. Our approach employs an agent to learn a policy that dynamically selects an action from a set of discrete epsilon-adjustment rules. This selection is based on a state representation that captures the population's convergence and feasibility status. A composite reward function is designed to quantify the improvement in the population's Pareto dominance and its proximity to the feasible region. Based on this reward signal, the agent autonomously balances the trade-off between feasibility and optimality across different evolutionary stages. Experimental results on 11 MW benchmark problems show that the proposed method achieves the best IGD values on 9 out of 11 test instances and maintains an average Friedman rank of 1.36, outperforming five competing algorithms. These results demonstrate that autonomous policy learning can effectively adapt constraint handling to problem-specific characteristics in CMOPs.

title: LSTM-Based Predictive Stock Selection for ESG Portfolio Optimization in the Malaysian Stock Market
authors: Rattapon Sriwichupong, Siriratch Sirisomphobchai, Nichapa Sirisakdanusorn, Jeeraporn Ananpraphakorn, Rujira Chaysiri
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: This study investigates the financial viability of sustainable investments using a machine learning-based framework to evaluate ESG-screened and conventional portfolios in the Malaysian stock market. A Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network is employed for daily return forecasting, which is integrated into three quarterly rebalanced portfolio optimizations: Mean-Variance (MV), Black-Litterman (BL), and Equal Weight (1/N). Results from a one-year out-ofsample test indicate that portfolio performance weakens after accounting for transaction costs. Nevertheless, the ESG portfolio demonstrates greater resilience than the Non-ESG and Mixed portfolios across all approaches. In particular, the ESG portfolio exhibits lower volatility and smaller maximum drawdowns. While the Non-ESG portfolio occasionally achieves higher risk-adjusted metrics, it is associated with larger drawdowns, indicating higher exposure to adverse market conditions. Overall, the results support the role of ESG integration in enhancing portfolio resilience in an emerging market context.

title: Power-Law Attention Module: An Adaptive Non-Linear Approach to Feature Map Refinement
authors: Thant Lwin Oo, Praisan Padungweang
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Attention mechanisms are used in Convolutional Neural Networks to focus on salient features. Unlike conventional attention modules that rely on linear scaling and straightforward activation functions, the proposed Power-Law Attention Module integrates trainable power-law exponents directly into the feature maps transformation as an attention mechanism. In this strategy, the power exponents are predicted and constrained within appropriate, learnable bounds. We then perform a power-law transformation on the intensity values of the respective feature maps. Finally, the gating mechanism adaptively combines the original features with their power-law transformed features, allowing the model to capture complex features. The experimental results on multiple real-world medical imaging datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the Power-Law Attention Module for medical image analysis.

title: Design and Implementation of a Digital Twin-Enhanced Collaborative Robot for Applied Robotics Education in STEM Environments
authors: Dennis Bolaños, Christian Caizaluisa, Daniel Zapata-Hidalgo, David Loza-Matovelle
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: This study presents the design and implementation of a collaborative robot (cobot) intended as an educational tool for robotics within the STEM framework. The cobot integrates direct and inverse kinematics for motion control and joint-level mechanical impedance modulation. It also features a user-friendly interface with a digital twin. The prototype was fabricated using additive manufacturing and optimized via CAD/CAE tools. It achieved a positioning accuracy of ±6.19 mm, which was validated under ISO 9283 standards. The digital twin serves not only as a technical support tool but also as a teaching resource, providing immediate visualization of robot states and motion. This helps learners connect kinematic and control concepts with observable behavior. The system supports autonomous ”Learning” and ”Reproduce” functions, bridging the gap between theoretical concepts and practical applications. These modes enable repeatable experimentation, allowing students to record and reproduce trajectories and to contrast the physical execution with its synchronized virtual representation. This reinforces understanding through guided trial and improvement. The Cobot involves learners in fundamental STEM disciplines, including computer programming, engineering design principles, mathematical concepts, and systems-oriented thinking. The collaborative robot has significant potential as an effective pedagogical platform that promotes interdisciplinary education and practical problem-solving in robotics. Finally, statistical analysis confirmed the normality of the data and the reliability of the performance data, further supporting its value in learning environments.

title: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems Information for Authors
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems

title: Parasitic-Aware Design of a 0.4–6-GHz Quadrature LO Generator in 14-nm FinFET
authors: Nam-Seog Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: This article presents a wideband quadrature local oscillator generator (LO-GEN) in 14-nm FinFET, achieving 175% frequency tuning range (FTR) (0.4–6 GHz) for 5G multicarrier systems. A hybrid architecture combines programmable dividers (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\div 2$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–8) for 0.4–3.3 GHz and an injection-locked frequency multiplier (ILFM) for 3.3–6.0 GHz using a single inductor–capacitor digitally controlled oscillator (LC-DCO). The key contribution is a rigorous analytical framework quantifying parasitic penalty factors in advanced-node injection circuits: we derive and experimentally validate that source degeneration (SD) injection achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$K \approx 1$</tex-math> </inline-formula> versus <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$K \approx 3$</tex-math> </inline-formula> for conventional Cascode topologies during triode-region operation, providing essential design guidelines for FinFET-based high-speed dividers. In addition, aggressive dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS) (0.8–0.6 V) serves a dual purpose—achieving 20% power reduction while extending divider locking range by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> via a process, voltage, and temperature (PVT)-robust lookup table without dedicated calibration. Software-managed frequency planning mitigates oscillator pulling in 98.5% of 3GPP 5-carrier scenarios. Occupying 0.154 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, the chip demonstrates −50.8-dBc integrated phase noise (IPN) (0.242-ps jitter) at 6 GHz and >49.2-dB image rejection ratio (IRR), and achieves a state-of-the-art figure of merit with tuning range (FoMT) of 210.4 dBc/Hz among wideband LO generators.

title: DPR-PIM: Diagnosis and Pipelined Repair Architecture for Processing-in-Memory
authors: Yunje Hwang, Younghun Kang, Hayoung Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Digital processing-in-memory (PIM) architectures have garnered significant attention for their ability to overcome the memory bottleneck in artificial intelligence (AI) acceleration. However, unlike conventional logic accelerators, dynamic random access memory (DRAM)-based digital PIMs tightly integrate compute and memory arrays within a single chip. This close coupling makes any logic fault directly affect stored data, leading to more critical system-level failures. Moreover, the coexistence of logic and memory components significantly increases testing complexity and fault localization difficulty. As AI is increasingly adopted in mission-critical domains such as autonomous driving, healthcare, and finance, the tolerance for computational errors continues to decline. In PIM-based AI accelerators, permanent faults originating from manufacturing processes can accumulate and propagate over repeated computations, making reliability and precision critical in safety-sensitive applications. To ensure high reliability, precise and independent fault diagnosis of all arithmetic units (AUs), including multipliers and adders, is essential. The proposed DPR-PIM architecture addresses this critical need by enabling complete per-unit diagnosis and fault localization. The DPR-PIM fully utilizes standard automatic test equipment (ATE) interfaces used in conventional memory testing, making it a practical and production-compatible solution. Moreover, DPR-PIM enables pipelined repair for single faults in adder stages based on accurate diagnosis results and applies fault blocking to prevent error propagation when multiple faults occur within the same adder stage or when faults occur in multipliers. By combining high test coverage, reliable repair mechanisms, and minimal area overhead, DPR-PIM offers a robust and scalable architecture for next-generation digital PIM systems.

title: Human–Machine Learning System for Demand Side Management and Carbon Emission Control in Commercial Buildings
authors: Jiatong Wang, Dillip Kumar Mishra, Rong Zhu, Wenyuan Bai, Kashem Muttaqi, Danny Sutanto, Li Li
abstract: Building energy consumption has taken up to 40% of global energy use, which substantially increases the resources for greenhouse gas emissions. Among various sectors, the commercial building sector is inevitably more favorable as the pioneer for decarbonization under the current power system structure due to the high peak electricity demand and inefficient energy management policy. This study investigates an efficient strategy for coordinating energy usage for commercial buildings to enhance the power system decarbonization and energy efficiency. First, this article introduces an Office Energy Management system for a grid-connected office building, incorporating electric vehicles, batteries, a lighting system, and a heating, ventilation, and air conditioning system. Then, an adjusted carbon emission flow model for dynamically tracking the carbon footprint is presented to fit the commercial building scenario. Finally, a novel human–machine learning (HML) system, combining human intelligence and reinforcement learning methods, is formulated to optimize energy management strategies. Unlike the traditional artificial intelligence technique, the HML paradigm enables humans and machines to collaborate at the energy management level, significantly improving algorithm performance. The proposed hybrid approach, using the HML method, offers up to 8.54% higher net benefits than the other state-of-the-art reinforcement approach.

title: Research on Wind and Airflow Angle Estimation Methods Based on GEM-CKS During Airflow Angle Measurement
authors: Kai Guan, Li Chen
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Aerospace Engineering (ICMTAE)
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of estimating wind and airflow angles during measurements without airflow angles. By combining aerodynamic models and aircraft kinematic relationships, a corresponding estimation model is established. Considering the issue that the estimation performance of existing Kalman filter-based methods depends on the noise intensity settings, this paper proposes a solution that uses a volume Kalman smoother to estimate the aircraft state variables and a generalized expectation maximization algorithm to estimate the process noise and measurement noise intensities. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed estimation method achieves high estimation accuracy, outperforming traditional volume Kalman filtering and volume Kalman smoothing algorithms, and is suitable for addressing wind and airflow angle estimation issues in scenarios without airflow angle measurements.

title: Explainable Neural Style Transfer Through Layer Wise Feature Attribution in VGG Networks
authors: Ankita Saha, Ayushmaan Dutta, Niladri Sanyal, Shubhra Samanta, Ratna Chakrabarty, Rajib Ghosh
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Neural Style Transfer generates artistic images by combining the content of one image with the style of another using deep neural networks. However, NST methods offer limited information about the contribution of different layers in final image formation. This paper proposes an explainable neural style transfer framework based on layer-wise feature attribution in a VGG-19 network. By conducting ablation studies, the contribution of individual layers to artistic style is analysed. Experimental results indicate that mid-level convolutional layers play a major role in capturing artistic textures, while shallow and deeper layers influence colour and global structure respectively.

title: Seeing Isn't Always Believing: Analysis of Grad-CAM Faithfulness and Localization Reliability in Lung Cancer CT Classification
authors: Teerapong Panboonyuen
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) techniques, such as Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (GradCAM), have become indispensable for visualizing the reasoning process of deep neural networks in medical image analysis. Despite their popularity, the faithfulness and reliability of these heatmap-based explanations remain under scrutiny. This study critically investigates whether Grad-CAM truly represents the internal decision-making of deep models trained for lung cancer image classification. Using the publicly available IQ-OTH/NCCD dataset, we evaluate five representative architectures - ResNet50, ResNet-101, DenseNet-161, EfficientNet-B0, and ViT-Base-Patch16-224 - to explore model-dependent variations in GradCAM interpretability. We introduce a quantitative evaluation framework that combines localization accuracy, perturbationbased faithfulness, and explanation consistency to assess GradCAM reliability across architectures. Experimental findings reveal that while Grad-CAM effectively highlights salient tumor regions in most convolutional networks, its interpretive fidelity significantly degrades for Vision Transformer models due to nonlocal attention behavior. Furthermore, cross-model comparison indicates substantial variability in saliency localization, implying that Grad-CAM explanations may not always correspond to the true diagnostic evidence used by the networks. This work exposes critical limitations of current saliency-based XAI approaches in medical imaging and emphasizes the need for model-aware interpretability methods that are both computationally sound and clinically meaningful. Our findings aim to inspire a more cautious and rigorous adoption of visual explanation tools in medical AI, urging the community to rethink what it truly means to “trust” a model's explanation.

title: A Deep Learning Framework Based on DenseNet121 for Cassava Leaf Disease Classification
authors: Parul Nasra, Jayapal Lande, Neha Sharma, Ashwani Kharola, Ritika Badhani
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Millions of people depend on cassava for their livelihoods and food security, especially in tropical and subtropical areas. However, leaf diseases include cassava mosaic disease (CMD), cassava brown streak disease (CBSD), cassava bacterial blight (CBB), and cassava green mite (CGM) negatively influence cassava farming, resulting in large crop losses and financial consequences. Traditional techniques of identifying diseases depend on specialist knowledge and manual inspection, which are subjective, time-consuming, and inappropriate for extensive surveillance. This study suggests a DenseNet121-driven deep learning framework for automated cassava leaf disease classification in order to overcome these drawbacks. To improve generalization, the suggested model combines efficient preprocessing methods including scaling, normalization, data augmentation, and class weighting with transfer learning using ImageNet-pretrained DenseNet121. The Kaggle Cassava Leaf Disease dataset, which consists of 21,367 photos in five classes, is used to assess the framework. The suggested method provides a classification accuracy of 91.6 % with balanced precision, recall, and F1-score across all disease categories, according to experimental results. Strong class-wise discrimination is seen in the confusion matrix, while training and validation curves verify steady convergence and little overfitting. The findings show that the suggested framework is reliable, scalable, and appropriate for practical precision agricultural applications, allowing for early disease identification and prompt management to boost cassava sustainability and productivity.

title: Emotion-Enriched Hybrid BERT for Depression and Anxiety Detection on Reddit: GoEmotions-Augmented Multi-Class Modeling
authors: Pranvendra Naruka, Simrin Syed, Madhu Shukla, Vipul Ladva, Neel Dholakia
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Depression and anxiety in social-media text are problematic because symptoms are diluted in short, noisy texts and the vocabulary of symptoms is crossed. In this paper, we introduce an emotion-augmented hybrid framework consisting of a transformer-based encoder (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, BERT) and fine-grained emotion signals encoded in alignment with the GoEmotions taxonomy. The model incorporates the [CLS] representation to a 28-dimensional emotion distribution and is end-to-end trained with multi-class classification of Reddit posts. In order to untangle the contribution of emotion cues for response, we also introduce sentiment-only and BERT-only ablations and robustness analysis through paired significance testing. The pipeline is replicable and privacy-friendly: It was executed on text-data alone and does not ask for user identifiers. No doctors set out on job replacement. This work contributes Sustainable Development Goal 3 (Good Health and Well-being), Target 3.4 of preventive mental health and well-being through making scalable early detection of mental-health risk from publicly shared text possible.

title: Semantic-Driven Facial Image Synthesis Using Enhanced Prompt Engineering and CLIP Guidance
authors: Abhijit J Patil, Kartik Deshmukh, Divya Ghanekar, Aishwarya Kewale
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: This paper presents a training-free baseline framework for text-to-face generation using pretrained Stable Diffusion and CLIP models. No additional model training or fine-tuning is performed. The proposed approach focuses on facial-specific prompt-engineering strategies and CLIP-based semantic evaluation to study text–image alignment in facial synthesis. Experimental evaluation on a limited set of facial descriptions demonstrates consistent semantic similarity with a CLIP score of 0.651, with an average generation time of under 15 seconds per image. Rather than competing with state-of-the-art methods, this work is intended as a reproducible baseline to support further research in semantic-driven facial image synthesis.

title: Empirical Study on the Impact of Different Pre-competition Tapering Models on Lower Limb Explosive Power in Latin Dance Athletes

title: Deep Learning in Resting-state Electroencephalogram Signals for Classification of Cognitively Healthy Older Adults, Alzheimer’s Disease and Lewy Body Dementia Patients
authors: Britman Salcedo Pumaccola, Claudio Babiloni, Francisco J. Fraga
abstract: This study investigates the application of deep learning techniques to resting-state electroencephalogram (rsEEG) signals for the classification of cognitively healthy older adults (NOLD), patients with Alzheimer’s disease (AD), and patients with Lewy body dementia (DLB). A dataset comprising 90 participants (30 AD, 30 DLB, and 30 NOLD) was analyzed, with AD and DLB groups further divided into mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and dementia (DEM) categories. Preprocessed rsEEG signals were decomposed into standard frequency bands, and normalized power spectral density (PSD) features were extracted from 19 channels. Two deep learning models — Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Transformer-based architectures — were implemented and validated using the Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) cross-validation scheme, to simulate real clinical generalization. The LSTM classifier achieved an average accuracy of 94.97%, with 95.03% sensitivity and 94.96% specificity across three binary group comparisons (AD vs. DLB, AD vs. NOLD, and DLB vs. NOLD) and three binary category comparisons (DEM vs. MCI, DEM vs. CTRL, and MCI vs. CTRL). The Transformer model achieved even higher performance, with an average accuracy of 96.40%, sensitivity of 97.29%, and specificity of 95.56%. In multi-class classification, both approaches demonstrated robust accuracy: 86.67% (LSTM) and 87.78% (Transformer) for five-class discrimination (AD-DEM, AD-MCI, DLB-DEM, DLB-MCI, and NOLD-CTRL), and above 90% for the three-class group (AD, DLB, and NOLD) and category (DEM, MCI, and CTRL) distinctions. These results demonstrate that deep learning applied to rsEEG data can effectively distinguish between AD, DLB, MCI, and healthy aging, offering a non-invasive, accurate, and generalizable method for early detection and differential detection of dementia-related disorders.

title: An Artificial Piezoelectric Whisker Sensor for Hydrodynamic Disturbance Detection
authors: Kecheng Zhang, Bowen Dong, Siyuan Wang, Hengxu Du, Xingfu Wan, Fanqing Hu, Yuxin Cui, Jianhua Liu, Peng Xu
abstract: Aquatic organisms have evolved highly sophisticated sensory systems over tens of thousands of years. Among these, the whisker array of seal is capable of sensitively detecting underwater flow information, providing crucial support for survival and predation in complex hydrodynamic environments. Inspired by the sensory mechanism of seal whisker, this study proposed an artificial piezoelectric whisker sensor (PWS), aimed at enhancing the flow perception capabilities of underwater vehicles in complex underwater environments. The PWS consists of an elliptical whisker shaft, a carbon fiber supporting rod, four sensing units, and a base, and is capable of effectively detecting wake characteristics generated by a propeller, including flow velocity and the general wake extent. Experimental results show that the PWS exhibits excellent sensitivity to variations in flow velocity, with a linear fitting correlation coefficient of the velocity output response consistently exceeding 0.99. Moreover, the sensor array based on the PWS shows strong perception and identification performance in artificial dipole source localization experiments. When integrated into an underwater vehicle platform, the PWS demonstrates stable perception of external flow disturbances under real deployment conditions. Overall, due to its high sensitivity, linear response characteristics, and adaptability to underwater environments, the proposed artificial piezoelectric seal whisker sensor holds great potential for applications in underwater flow perception and intelligent underwater vehicle systems.

title: Suppression of Compound Mainlobe Interferences Exploiting Nonconvex Low-rank and Sparse Decomposition in FDA-MIMO Radar
authors: Qikai Wang, Lan Lan, Shengqi Zhu, Ximin Li, Guisheng Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper addresses the issue of suppressing com pound mainlobe interferences in frequency diverse array multiple input multiple-output (FDA-MIMO) radar, including the rapidly generated jammers as well as burst interference, based on noncon vex low-rank and sparse decomposition (NLRSD). In particular, the problem is formulated by leveraging the low-rank characteristics of deceptive jammers and the true target, as well as sparse char acteristic of burst interference in the spatial-temporal domain. To address the problem, a NLRSD method is developed, exploiting the unitary invariance of the γ norm and the Moreau-Yosida operator to reformulate the problem as a singular value optimization task. The alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) is implemented to separate the target and interference signals. Furthermore, the convergence performance, which satisfies the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions, as well as the computational complexity of the proposed NLRSD-ADMM algorithm are rigorously analyzed. Numerical results and comparisons with existing frameworks and methods validate the effectiveness of the proposed NLRSD-ADMM method in suppressing both rapidly generated mainlobe jammers and burst interference.

title: Effects of Belt Accelerations During Push-Off on Propulsion Mechanics in Individuals Post-Stroke
authors: Hannah N. Cohen, Tamara Wright, GilHwan Kim, Henry Wright, Darcy S. Reisman, Fabrizio Sergi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Stroke often causes hemiparesis, affecting balance and walking ability. Propulsion, a major subtask of walking, has two components: trailing limb posture and propulsive force generated by plantarflexor muscles. Our group previously developed a method to challenge propulsion by accelerating the belt supporting the trailing limb during push off. In this study, we test the efficacy of a similar paradigm in 34 post-stroke individuals, and compare the effects of posterior belt accelerations applied to both legs (symmetric condition), and only to the paretic leg (asymmetric condition). We hypothesized that the two conditions would elicit changes in propulsion mechanics during and after exposure. Results indicate that belt accelerations induced measurable effects in paretic propulsion mechanics during exposure, and some of these effects persisted over a 1-3 minute post-exposure session conducted at self-selected speed. Specifically, by the end of the exposure session, participants increased their paretic TLA by 7.2 ± 0.9%, and their plantarflexor muscle activation by 6.6 ± 2.2% in the soleus and 7.8 ± 2.3% in the lateral gastrocnemius, compared to their baseline. Changes in propulsion mechanics led to a small but statistically significant (0.024 m/s or 3.4 ± 1.4%) post-exposure increase in self-selected walking speed. Effects were primarily induced on metrics of propulsion mechanics of the leg directly exposed to belt acceleration; therefore, differential effects as a function of acceleration condition were only observed for the non-paretic leg. A responder analysis indicated that individuals with greater impairment exhibited larger relative changes in plantarflexor muscle activation after exposure.

title: Analysis of Loss Reductions in SiC Automotive Traction Inverters Using Pulse-to-Pulse Active Gate Drivers
authors: Michael J. Frank, Mark-M. Bakran
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: In electric vehicle powertrains, standard driving cycles are dominated by partial-load conditions in which conventional gate drivers operate inefficiently. This paper investigates the potential for loss reduction in silicon carbide (SiC) automotive traction inverters using pulse-to-pulse active gate drivers (AGDs) that dynamically adapt to switching current and junction temperature. While various AGD methodologies exist, a rigorous quantitative comparison of different topologies remains limited. This study analyzes traction inverter losses under gate resistance manipulation (GRM) and gate voltage manipulation (GVM) strategies with varying discrete control resolutions (two, three, and infinite), benchmarked against the theoretical minimum achievable losses. Control parameters are adapted exclusively between switching pulses (“pulse-to-pulse”) to minimize implementation complexity. Special attention is given to capacitive charging processes to isolate real channel switching losses from measured energy, a critical correction for accuracy at low loads. The analysis links loss reduction capabilities to fundamental power semiconductor principles, revealing a distinct performance trade-off: GVM outperforms GRM during turn-on, while GRM proves superior during turn-off switching. Furthermore, the study demonstrates that simple two-level control strategies capture the vast majority of theoretical savings, yielding diminishing returns for higher-resolution control. The impact of increased conduction losses due to reduced on-state gate voltages in GVM topologies is quantified and proven negligible. Finally, the loss model is experimentally verified via thermal measurements on an H-bridge in continuous operation, confirming the predicted efficiency gains.

title: Development of an Upgraded Sarrus-Driven Mobile Robot for Height Adaptability and Environmental Functionality
authors: Phuong Thao Thai, Le Quan Hoang
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
abstract: Transformable mobile robots are interesting topics for researchers for their high mobility and adaptability that can be applied in various environment explorations and military. This paper introduces a novel transformable mobile robot capable of height adjustment and mobility in different path and working spaces. The suggested robot features a Sarrus mechanism mounted on a mobile platform capable of transforming from a flat to a cubic configuration. Sarrus mechansim can alter the shape by 1 motor, and the adjustment of the robot's height from 3 cm to 9 cm. The robot's mobility involves trajectory tracking, managing both linear and curved paths. The capacity for height adjustment and mobility of this Sarrus mobile robot has been considered and computed by MATLAB and Simscape with high accuracy.

title: Active Surveillance of Respiratory Viruses in Influenza-Like Illness and Severe Acute Respiratory Infection Following the COVID-19 Pandemic in Viet Nam
authors: Vu Mai Phuong Hoang, Le Khanh Hang Nguyen, Thi Thanh Le, Thi Hong Trang Ung, Vu Son Nguyen, Duc Cuong Vuong, Phuong Anh Nguyen, Thi Thu Huong Tran, Thi Quynh Mai Le
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
abstract: After the COVID-19 pandemic, the influenza-like illness (ILI) and severe acute respiratory infection (SARI) surveillance system in Vietnam was disrupted. From November 2024, the study was conducted with the objective detecting influenza viruses, SARS-CoV-2, and Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in ILI and respiratory viruses in SARI cases in Viet Nam. The surveillance system comprises seven sentinels located in Northern (three sentinels), Central (two sentinels), and Southern (two sentinels) regions. Nasopharyngeal swabs were collected and performed real-time RT-PCR with specific primers and probes for screening and subtyping respiratory viruses. From November 2024 to May 2025, 1632 ILI and 1224 SARI samples were collected. The ILI positive rate was 32.0% (522/1632) showing influenza A/H3 (13.6%), A/H1pdm09 (10.8%), influenza B Victoria (2.8%), 4.3% RSV and 0,5% SARS-CoV-2. SARI cases exhibited 53,5% (656/1224) with prominent influenza A/H3 16.6% (203/1224), influenza B Victoria 9.3%, A/H1pdm09 7.2%; other viruses, RSV and hMPV reached 6.3% each pathogen, 3.3% Adenovirus, 0.6% SARS-CoV-2. Children 0-5 years old with SARI had the rate of RSV 10.6% (72/677), hMPV <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10.7 \%(73 / 677)$</tex>, and Adenovirus <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.6 \%(31 / 677)$</tex>. Coinfections were observed in children and elderly SARI (3.9%) in that Adenovirus co-infected with other viruses in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50 \%$</tex> of cases. Over six months, influenza viruses were the main pathogen in November and December 2024. Influenza B Yamagata did not appear; SARS-CoV-2 showed low circulation during this period. Temporal analysis revealed distinct circulation patterns for influenza, RSV, and SARS-CoV-2, with influenza peaking earlier. The higher influenza positivity, RSV, and hMPV cocirculation in SARI underscore their potential to cause severe respiratory illness, particularly in young children. The resumption of active ILI and SARI surveillance systems is necessary to monitor the circulation of respiratory viruses which contributes to developing effective measures for protection and prevention strategies in Viet Nam.
