title: Tariff-Aware Multi-EV Scheduling for Residential Communities Using V2H/V2V Under Specific Demand Boundaries
authors: Vaishnavi Suresh Yadao, Sachin Kumar Jain
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The growing number of electric vehicles (EVs) poses several issues for residential electricity networks, especially when unregulated charging exceeds safe operating limits for site demand and raises daily energy expenses. An efficient scheduling system for numerous EVs that controls multiple chargers based on real Indian time-of-day tariff’s is presented in this paper. In order to maintain a minimum SoC and to keep site demand limit under certain present level, the system uses charging power among fixed, multilevel, and continuous chargers. Vehicle-to-home (V2H) export and vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) energy transfer are incorporated into the architecture to reduce peak demand and improve battery efficiency. Upon arrival, each EV is fully charged using a baseline “First-Come-First-Serve” method for direct comparison. According to simulation results employing seasonal base-load profiles, the proposed strategy successfully lowers peak site demand and energy costs without going against the SoC targets. This suggests a clear-cut and useful approach to EV integration in residential settings.

title: Preparation and Static Calibration Study of Flexible Thin-Film Thermocouples
authors: Zhuowen Wan
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Aerospace Engineering (ICMTAE)
abstract: This study designed and fabricated a multilayer thinfilm thermocouple comprising a polyimide (PI) substrate, nickelsilicon (NiSi) and nickel-chromium (NiCr) electrodes, and a silicon dioxide (SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) protective layer. This structure exhibits high thermoelectric sensitivity, excellent mechanical flexibility, and environmental stability. To evaluate its performance, systematic static calibration was conducted within the 0-260 °C temperature range using a controlled experimental platform. The fabricated device exhibited a stable Seebeck coefficient (average value 0.0387 mV/°C) within the K-type thermocouple standard range, demonstrating excellent linearity (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>>0.99) and repeatability over five calibration cycles. These results validate the thin-film thermocouple's high-precision temperature measurement capability. With its minimal thermal mass, rapid response, and robust mechanical properties, the device demonstrates strong integration potential for wearable systems, health monitoring platforms, and flexible electronics applications.

title: VirtuScribe – Brain-to-Text Intelligent Note-TakingAssistant for Meetings
authors: G.Pallavi Reddy, I. Sudha
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Capturing meeting details in real time often relies on speech-to-text tools or manual note-taking. These methods can be slow, error-prone, and distracting. To address this, VirtuScribe, a brain-computer interface, uses deep learning models like LSTM and Transformers to convert EEG brain signals into structured notes. The system performs preprocessing, noise reduction, feature extraction, and NLP summarization, producing clear documentation with minimal delay. VirtuScribe achieves over $90 \%$ transcription accuracy with low latency, ensuring reliability and efficiency. Its applications include corporate meetings, classrooms, and assistive technologies, aiding people with physical disabilities. Unlike traditional methods requiring speaking or writing, VirtuScribe provides a silent, hands-free, inclusive way to record discussions, advancing the future of meeting documentation.

title: Development of Porous $\pi$-Conjugated Polymer Films for Organic Field-Effect Transistor-Based Sensors in High-Performance Ammonia Detection at Human Breath Levels
authors: Vinh Van Tran, Truong Thi Vu Nu, Viet Q. Bui, Mincheol Chang
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
abstract: Ammonia <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{NH}_{3}\right)$</tex> is a promising disease-specific biomarker for non-invasive early diagnosis and continuous health monitoring. The development of electronic sensors capable of detecting <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{NH}_{3}$</tex> at parts-per-billion (ppb) levels has emerged as a highly attractive and actively explored avenue within the Health Science and Technology community. In this work, we report the detection of ultra-low concentrations of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{NH}_{3}$</tex> using an organic field-effect transistor (OFET) sensor, highlighting its potential for breath-based ammonia sensing. This highly sensitive OFET sensor is fabricated using a facile strategy that modulates the morphological and chargetransport properties of nanoporous poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) films. The fabrication method involves shearingassisted phase separation of polymer blends, followed by selective etching to generate a well-defined nanoporous architecture. The resulting films feature nanoscale dimensions, with pore sizes and thicknesses of approximately 90 nm and 7 nm, respectively-both of which can be precisely tuned by adjusting the shear rate. The sensor's performance was evaluated across a range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{NH}_{3}$</tex> concentrations, achieving a limit of detection as low as 500 ppb and demonstrating good selectivity for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{NH}_{3}$</tex> over various organic solvent vapors. Comprehensive analysis of the morphology and electrical properties of the CP films reveals those morphological features-such as film thickness and surface area-have a more pronounced influence on sensing performance than intrinsic charge-transport characteristics. The porosity of the P3HT films enables more effective interaction with analyte molecules, while reduced film thickness shortens diffusion paths, thereby enhancing both response time and sensitivity. Importantly, this highly efficient room-temperature sensor, capable of detecting <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{NH}_{3}$</tex> at very low concentrations, shows significant promise for applications in early detection of kidney-related diseases and environmental monitoring

title: A Robust Residual Autoencoder Framework for Anomaly-Based Network Intrusion Detection
authors: Nouman Ijaz, Sana Ullah Jan, Insoo Koo
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Advancements in Computational Sciences (ICACS)
abstract: The growing complexity of network environments and the rapid emergence of zero-day attacks have undermined the effectiveness of traditional signature-based intrusion detection systems (IDS). To address these limitations, this paper proposes an autoencoder framework, termed a Residual Autoencoder (RAE), for unsupervised anomaly-based intrusion detection. The proposed model is trained solely on benign traffic and detects abnormal behavior by computing reconstruction errors on unseen data. This is achieved by adding a residual skip connection to maintain information of low-level features and stabilize gradient flow during training. Furthermore, a hybrid loss, which is a combination of Smooth L1 loss and cosine similarity, simultaneously applies numerical precision and structural consistency between the input and the reconstructed features. The experiments using the UNSW-NB15 dataset show that the proposed RAE is an effective framework to differentiate between normal and attack traffic, with an ROC-AUC score of 0.903, surpassing baseline methods, including the plain autoencoder (0.884), isolation forest (0.860), and one-class SVM networks (0.820). The results indicate that the model can effectively handle new and zero-day attacks. Additionally, it offers a robust, data-efficient, and scalable solution for next-generation network intrusion detection systems.

title: Parallel Clustering-Splitting Federated Learning for Heterogeneous AIoT
authors: Jingyi Chen, Qiang Wang
abstract: In Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT), the rapid growth of connected devices, data volume, and complex model architectures make heterogeneous, and latency requirements remain persistent challenges. The split federated learning (SFL) mitigates these challenges through model splitting and client-side parallel processing. However, how to jointly optimize model accuracy and latency based on adaptive clustering and splitting remains a significant research challenge under a heterogeneous scenario. Considering system and data heterogeneity jointly, we propose a cluster-based cloud-edge-end dynamic collaborative SFL architecture. We define the clustering and splitting factors to evaluate the quality of the clustering and splitting decisions. Then we propose a parallel clustering-splitting federated learning algorithm (PCSFL) to achieve a trade-off between model accuracy and latency. The clustering and splitting decisions are performed in parallel. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the superiority of PCSFL in both global accuracy and average wait time across heterogeneous AIoT.

title: Improved Gaussian Wasserstein Distance: A Smooth Adaptive New Metric for Remote Sensing Tiny Object Detection
authors: Heng Hu, Si-Bao Chen, Jin Tang
abstract: Due to tiny size of tiny objects, which contain only a small number of pixels and lack of appearance information, it is difficult for detectors to recognize such objects. Through experimental analysis, it is found that existing metrics are misfit and not applicable to remote sensing tiny object detection. Among them, Intersection over Union (IoU) series of metrics are extremely sensitive to slight offset of position of tiny objects. Distance metrics such as Dot Distance (DotD), Gaussian Wasserstein Distance (GWD), Normalized Wasserstein Distance (NWD) and Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KLD) either do not have scale invariance, are not symmetric, or are unable to measure probability distribution of two non-overlapping regions, which leads to their limitations when applied to remotely sensed tiny objects as well. Based on this, we propose Improved Gaussian Wasserstein Distance (IGWD). This metric not only mitigates position offset sensitivity problem, but also solves some of problems of distance metrics. IGWD has a good generalization ability and can be easily embedded into other detectors. In addition, our improved KLD metric also has good generalization ability and its performance outperforms existing metrics. Meanwhile, in order to solve problems of channel isolation of traditional convolution and computational redundancy and weight bias of attention mechanism, we propose Adaptive Channel Graph Convolution (ACGC). Based on idea of graph convolution, feature interactions between channels are effectively enhanced by fusing standard convolution with graph convolution. Ultimately, we combine the new metrics with ACGC to construct the YOLO-ACGC detector for remote sensing of tiny objects, and carry out a large number of experiments on several public datasets to verify effectiveness of method.

title: Low-Power Low-Noise SiPM Readout Preamplifier for Harsh Environments
authors: Tom Klauner, Ottilie Bonfanti, Nicolas Roisin, Denis Flandre
abstract: A low-power, low-noise first-stage transimpedance amplifier (TIA) for silicon photomultiplier (SiPM) readout has been developed for harsh environments, targeting low Earth orbit (LEO) CubeSat applications. The circuit ensures reliable operation up to a total ionizing dose (TID) of 160 krad(SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>) and with temperatures ranging from -55 °C to +125 °C. The TIA employs a folded-cascode architecture implemented in a 0.18 μm bulk CMOS process. It includes a low-voltage cascode bias circuit that is specifically designed to maintain functionality under shifts in the MOSFET electrical parameters induced by irradiation and temperature variations. This study combines simulation and experimental characterization to evaluate the readout performance of the TIA using a commercial Hamamatsu S13360-1375 SiPM. Under nominal conditions, the measured amplifier achieves a bandwidth of 4.6 MHz when connected to the SiPM, with a power consumption of 238 μW. The TIA also shows a measured input-referred noise of 61.0 nA<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub> when connected to an optimal input capacitance of 12 pF. Measurements confirm that the readout operation is maintained without significant degradation after irradiation and across the full temperature range, validating its suitability for LEO applications.

title: Optimizing Drug Management: Enhancing Pharmacy Analytics and Electronic Health Records with Explainable AI and Graph Neural Networks
authors: Samit Karmakar, Chandan Kumar Mahato, Sourav Pandey, Sutapa Ray, Soumik Kumar Kundu, Md Moniruzzaman
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: The paper probe an improved upon AI-Driven approach to pharmacy analytics with Electronics Health Records (EHRs) by introducing XGBoost combined with SHAP for explainable medicine recommendation and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to predict drug-drugs interactions. The paper presents the growth and evaluation of an AI-powered medicine managements system unification SHAPs-based model interpretability and GNNs-based drug-drug interaction prediction to safeguard patient health. This research look at these models on public data and pretends to use healthcare data, achieving good accuracy, interpretability, and safety. The future work previous efforts by combining interpretability and safety into practical, patient pharmacy management.

title: Continuous Current-Mode Class-F Power Amplifiers: Theory and Experimental Validation
authors: Daniel Alonso-Tejera, J. Apolinar Reynoso-Hernández, José Raúl Loo-Yau, Manuel Alejandro Pulido-Gaytán, María del Carmen Maya-Sánchez, Jaime Sánchez-García, Eduardo Antonio Murillo-Bracamontes
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This extended article presents the theoretical development and experimental validation of the continuous current-mode (CCM) Class-F power amplifier (PA), as an alternate amplifier mode to alleviate the dynamic breakdown problem in Class-F continuous-mode (CM) PAs, observed in low-breakdown-voltage technologies such as gallium arsenide (GaAs). Building on our previous work, we develop the theory and experimental procedures to study Class-F PAs and their CM operation, highlighting how voltage spikes limit their applicability in certain circumstances. We propose the CCM Class-F approach, which modifies the current waveform to avoid excessive voltage spikes, preserving efficiency while attempting to extend bandwidth. In addition, details of the time-domain three-harmonic active load–pull system are presented with special emphasis on the calibration procedure of the low-frequency nonlinear vector network analyzer (LF-NVNA) system used for measuring the waveforms at the intrinsic current generator plane of the device. Experimental validation on GaAs and gallium nitride (GaN) devices demonstrates that the CCM Class-F design eliminates soft breakdown in GaAs and remains relevant in GaN, enabling the workspace extension without compromising device efficiency or reliability.

title: Fast and Robust Deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Han Jiao, Jiakai Sun, Lei Zhao, Zhanjie Zhang, Wei Xing, Huaizhong Lin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting has demonstrated remarkable real-time rendering capabilities and superior visual quality in novel view synthesis for static scenes. Building upon these advantages, researchers have progressively extended 3D Gaussians to dynamic scene reconstruction. Deformation field-based methods have emerged as a promising approach among various techniques. These methods maintain 3D Gaussian attributes in a canonical field and employ the deformation field to transform this field across temporal sequences. Nevertheless, these approaches frequently encounter challenges such as suboptimal rendering speeds, significant dependence on initial point clouds, and vulnerability to local optima in dim scenes. To overcome these limitations, we present FRoG, an efficient and robust framework for high-quality dynamic scene reconstruction. FRoG integrates per-Gaussian embedding with a coarse-to-fine temporal embedding strategy, accelerating rendering through the early fusion of temporal embeddings. Moreover, to enhance robustness against sparse initializations, we introduce a novel depth- and error-guided sampling strategy. This strategy populates the canonical field with new 3D Gaussians at low-deviation initial positions, significantly reducing the optimization burden on the deformation field and improving detail reconstruction in both static and dynamic regions. Furthermore, by modulating opacity variations, we mitigate the local optima problem in dim scenes, improving color fidelity. Comprehensive experimental results validate that our method achieves accelerated rendering speeds while maintaining state-of-the-art visual quality.

title: Bi-layer Cartesian Back-Projection Focusing Algorithm and Airborne Radio Echo Sounding Application for Antarctic Subglacial Structure Imaging
authors: Chen Lv, Wenhao Luo, Kenichi Matsuoka, Tong Hao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Radio echo sounding (RES), a powerful remote geophysical technique, provides excellent insight into the characterization of subglacial environment. However, existing time-domain RES imaging methods face challenges in balancing computational efficiency and imaging accuracy for along-track focusing of RES data. In this study, we propose a bi-layer Cartesian back-projection (BCBP) algorithm for efficient and accurate imaging of subglacial structures, which incorporates the newly derived medium-based spectrum compression within an interpolation-free imaging framework. The proposed approach inherently accounts for refractive effects in the Antarctic bi-layer imaging scenario and effectively mitigates spectrum aliasing under low-sampling subglacial conditions. It thereby eliminates the need for spatial interpolation during sub-image fusion to achieve accelerated computation without sacrificing focusing quality. The algorithm is validated by point-target simulation of a classical ice geometry and further applied to airborne RES data from two representative regions of Antarctica: the interior Gamburtsev region and the coastal fringe of Totten Glacier. Both simulation and field RES results demonstrate that the proposed BCBP algorithm achieves high-resolution focusing comparable to the benchmarking BP, while offering substantially improved computational efficiency. The successful validation of the BCBP algorithm underscores its potential for wide application in precise subglacial imaging by efficiently re-processing existing RES data, as well as new data to be acquired in the coming years in Antarctica.

title: VoteZAP- Blockchain-Based Secure Online Voting Systems: Architecture, Security, and Future Directions
authors: Nayan Rathi, Aakil Lulani, Shreyash Daga, Akarsh Meshram, Ashish Kumar Soni
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Election is one of the essential processes of democratic society, yet the traditional types of voting are still characterized by the inefficiency problems, absence of transparency, and the risk of frauds. The introduction of electronic and internet-based voting systems in an attempt to overcome some of these limitations, they are usually weak with regard to some critical challenges like authentication, secrecy of ballots, and resistance to cyberattacks. In this paper, I will suggest a blockchain-based secure online voting scheme that combined cryptographic tool and distributed ledger technology to implement end to end election security. The framework uses RSA to exchange key, compute hashing with SHA-512, and homomorphic encryption in order to allow tallying of votes without affecting the privacy of the voter. The votes are stored permanently in the blockchain, which is both transparent and immutable, hence, it is not tampered with. System analysis shows that the suggested model can counter such significant threats as denial-of-service (DoS) and man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks, and it guarantees receipt-freeness, resistance against coercions, and multiple votes. More so, blockchain integration makes elections more inclusive in that it minimizes administrative burden and remote participation is possible. This article adds to the body of literature on e-governance making blockchain technology a potential revolutionary platform of reliable, scalable, and affordable elections.

title: AI-Based Crowd Density Analysis for Railway Stations
authors: S Alex David, K. Yashwanthi, K. Harshitha, G. Shanmukh Pavan Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Controlling masses of people in busy areas such as railway stations and temples is becoming a challenge especially with reference to the safety of people and their free flow. This project presents an AI-based system to aid in monitoring and analyzing the crowd density as it happens. With the help of modern computer vision and the deep learning models (YOLO or Faster R-CNN), the system is capable of properly recognizing people in video feeds, including in a crowded environment. It then approximates the number of people present in a place and points out areas where there is a growing congestion. Introducing the concepts of heat maps and a real-time alarm, the authorities will be able to react fast to the crowd and mitigate risks as well as ensure the order in crowded places such as railway stations and temples with the help of this project based on artificial intelligence to analyze the crowd density. Using the deep learning approach on the videos input, the system is able to locate and calculate the number of people in real-time, which can be used to identify the overcrowded areas and manage the crowd better. The system, in general, provides a smart and effective way of monitoring the crowd levels in crowded places.

title: Research on an Evaluation System for Digital Literacy of Primary Education Majors Based on a PSO-SVM Model
authors: Jie Zhou, Yuan Xu
publish: 2025 21st International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Security (CIS)
abstract: With the advancement of educational informatization 2.0, enhancing the digital literacy of primary education majors has become imperative. This study addresses the limitations of traditional assessment methods in terms of efficiency and diagnostic precision by proposing a evaluation model based on Particle Swarm Optimization-Support Vector Machine (PSOSVM). A six-dimensional evaluation framework encompassing information literacy, technical application, content creation, communication and collaboration, digital ethics and safety, and learning attitudes was constructed. This study utilized empirical data gathered from university students enrolled in a primary education program. The diagnostic performance of the model was markedly improved by leveraging the PSO algorithm to autonomously fine-tune the pivotal parameters of the SVM. The experimental findings indicate that the PSO-SVM model attained a classification accuracy of 99.0%, outperforming traditional models. Feature importance analysis revealed that “technical application” and “learning attitude” are key factors influencing students' digital literacy. The model effectively identifies students' competency gaps, providing a scientific basis for personalized training and instructional interventions, with substantial practical application value.

title: Voice-Enabled Healthcare Assistance Systems for Elderly And Visually Impaired: A Comparative Analysis
authors: Riddhiman Rakshit, Nirvik Roy, Priyadarshi Gupta, Rajarshi Dhara, Akash Saha, Ayushi Banerjee, Soumik Kumar Kundu, Samit Karmakar, Rajib Ghosh
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Voice-controlled wearable health assistants are developing into key resources for enabling independent living practices respectively, among senior citizens and visually impaired individuals. The study performs a “A systematic comparison between a custom ESP32-based solution and the smartwatch prototype compared to five other watches: WatchOut Wearables (Kids & Guardian versions), Apple Watch Series 11, AngelSense GPS Tracker, WellBe Watch with Virtual Assistant, iHealth Breakthrough Wireless Smartwatch. The assessment tests the essentials including capabilities such as GPS tracking, health monitoring, fall detection, SOS alerts. calling, and voice control; accessibility features including tactile feedback, UI support for low vision users, and audio interaction; and smart services like AIpowered alerts, caregiver integration, and health data sharing. Technical specifications eSIM/4G, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, smartphone dependency and practical considerations such as battery life and cost of subscriptions was also investigated. The analysis revealed there were gaps, in particular the lack of rich, accessible voice interfaces for the visually impaired and the use of expensive subscription-based features. There is evidence from the analysis which supports the development of a systems, this work proposes a low-cost ESP32-based prototype for wearables to function independently using voice-controlled functionalities off-line and emergency functions combined assistance. The findings of this analysis will provide insights for the creation of future accessible, affordable, and user-centered healthcare wearables.

title: Machine Learning-Based Investigation of Temperature Effects on Lateral Migration in 3-D nand Flash Memory
authors: Insang Han, Hyungcheol Shin
abstract: In this study, we investigate the temperature effects of lateral migration (LM) in 3-D <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> flash memory using a machine learning (ML) approach. The LM is not only the dominant failure mechanism in long-term retention but also significantly degrades the lifetime estimation at room temperature (RT) due to its temperature dependence. Therefore, we model deep neural networks (DNNs) at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$25~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$55~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$85~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$115~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, and embed them into a Monte Carlo framework to predict and analyze the effects that are difficult to capture through measurements. In this process, cell variation (CV) and trap variation (TV) arising from limitations of the fabrication process, as well as pattern dependence (PD) from program data, are considered. To cover various conditions of retention time (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${t}_{\text {R}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> points and bit errors, the stretched exponential function (SEF) was employed, and the variations of its parameters with temperature are analyzed. A key finding of this study is that the extracted activation energy (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${E}_{\text {a}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> is nonconstant, depending on the error threshold and variation in CV, TV, and PD. The results from our methodology provide deeper insights into LM and also offer design guidelines for more accurate lifetime estimation in 3-D <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> flash memory.

title: Dual Layered Hybrid Blockchain Architecture – Securing Electronic Health Records (EHR) Using Blockchain Technology with AI Enabled EHR Data Analysis
authors: Samit Karmakar, Himanshu Shekhar, Piyush Rai, Souhardya Chowdhury, Srinjoy Bandyopadhyay, Ankana Roy, Soumik Kumar Kundu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: With rapid advancement in technology and digitisation of data, the end users have started to perform their work more efficiently, minimising their work time. The enhancement of Electronic Health Records (EHR) is one of the examples of this digitisation, enabling less paperwork. But this advancement in EHR has raised challenges over the security and privacy of the user and the medical institution's data. To keep it safe and reliable for the users and the medical institutions, integrating EHR with blockchain technology would be a proposed solution to the problem. The combination of decentralised blockchain properties with cryptographic methods enhances the security. This paper proposes hybrid blockchain-based electronic health records, a hybrid multilayer blockchain architecture using artificial intelligence which provides ease of self-sustained approaches to personal privacy through smart contracts by keeping data control in user hands and AI-based anomaly detection and predictive analytics.

title: Automated Malaria Detection Using VGG19: A Deep Learning Approach to Blood Smear Classification
authors: Saurabh Fulara, Kanegonda Ravi Chythanya, Neha Sharma, Chandra Kishore, Gaurav Tuteja
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Malaria remains a major global health challenge, particularly in regions with limited access to timely and accurate diagnostic tools. This study presents an automated malaria detection approach utilizing a deep learning-based convolutional neural network (CNN), specifically the VGG19 architecture, to classify parasitized and uninfected red blood cells from stained microscopic images. The model was fine-tuned and trained on a publicly available dataset consisting of 27,588 labeled cell images. Comprehensive preprocessing techniques were employed to normalize image quality, followed by model training using transfer learning and additional fully connected layers tailored for binary classification. Performance evaluation included metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with the model achieving a classification accuracy of 96.09%, precision of 97.21%, recall of 95.01%, and F 1 -score of 96.10%. Validation was further supported by visual analysis using confusion matrices and lossaccuracy plots across epochs. The proposed methodology demonstrates high reliability and efficiency, highlighting the potential of deep learning for real-time malaria diagnosis in clinical and field settings. The findings contribute to the growing body of research focused on AI-driven medical diagnostics and underscore the importance of scalable, automated solutions for disease identification in resource-constrained environments.

title: Lung-Retinanet: Lung Cancer Detection Using a Retina-Net with Multi-Scale Feature Fusion and Context Module
authors: J Srikanth, A. Sankar Reddy, K. Manasa Sri, V. Vinod, D. Sarika, D charan Sai
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Early identification is crucial for better results due to a high death rate from lung cancer. Various current techniques such as sputum cytology, CT scans, and X-rays too often fail to identify early-stage cancers correctly. Traditional machine learning methods, such as Random Forests and SVM, rely on manual feature extraction and cannot generalize their findings. To address this challenge, a deep learning model for automatic lung tumors detection and severity assessment was developed, known as Lung-RetinaNet. It incorporates adaptive anchors, a dilated lightweight context module, and a multi-scale feature fusion module to enhance the detection of small cancers. The goal of Lung-RetinaNet is to dramatically raise the detection precision of small lung cancers by incorporating context across network levels.

title: A Hybrid CART-Gaussian Process Framework for Day-Ahead Electricity Load Forecasting with Uncertainty Quantification
authors: Ei Thu Thu Tun, Chawalit Jeenanunta, Pyae Pyae Phyo, Kamal Chapagain
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Power and Renewable Energy Engineering (PREE)
abstract: Day-ahead electricity load forecasting (DALF) is critical for the planning, operation, and economic efficiency of modern power systems. However, load time series exhibit strong nonlinearity, volatility, and sensitivity to exogenous variables such as holidays, weekends, and temporal effects. To address these complexities, this paper presents a CART-based load grouping strategy combined with Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) for leaf-node forecasting. The proposed approach partitions the dataset using Calendar-based Classification and Regression Trees (CART) with split variables such as day of the week, holiday, and bridging holiday, producing homogeneous subgroups of load patterns. Within each subgroup, GPR models are trained on lagged load features and exogenous inputs, providing both point forecasts and probabilistic intervals. Empirical results using three years of actual load data (2019-2021) from the Electricity Generating Authority of Thailand (EGAT) demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high forecasting accuracy. Model performance is assessed through traditional accuracy metrics, including Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE), alongside interval reliability based on confidence interval coverage. Furthermore, the GPR confidence intervals provide calibrated uncertainty quantification, which is essential for operational decision-making under uncertainty.

title: Artificial Intelligence in the Financial Industry: A Review
authors: Harvey Weinstein C. Y., Laurens Spits Warnars, Christopher Sugianto K., Rudy Tjahyadi
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) technology in the last few years has led to a significant shift in how many industries operate. Automation, efficiency, and innovation are key factors that have led to the adoption of AI in many industries. In the financial industry, AI has been utilized for fraud detection, risk assessment, personalized financial advice, etc. This paper is a literature review on the components and challenges in the implementation of artificial intelligence in the financial industry. For the purpose of this research, we conducted a comprehensive study of the existing literature. We constructed a primary search string in the Scopus database focused on the implementation of AI in Fintech. Quality screenings were then performed and followed by thorough analysis. Out of the 2,425 studies, 27 primary studies published between 2021 and 2025 were chosen. Through this paper, we hope that our findings will offer practitioners, researchers, and institutions valuable insights for responsible and successful adoption of AI in the evolving digital financial landscape. Unlike prior reviews that focus on specific domains such as banking, robo-advisory services, or explainable AI in finance, this study systematically synthesizes implementation components and cross-cutting challenges of AI across the broader financial and fintech ecosystem for the 2021-2025 period. It develops an integrative framework and practical guidelines to support responsible, scalable AI adoption by financial institutions, regulators, and technology providers.

title: AI-Driven Cloud Resource Auto-Scaling in Multi-Cloud Environments Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Richa Sharma, Charanjit Singh, Prabhjot Kaur
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: The deployment of packages and scalability became a revolution with cloud computing. However, it is not easy to run auto-scaling with different multi-cloud systems. The paper suggests a reinforcement learning model as the tool to optimise wind of cloud resources in auto-scaling through platforms with a trade-off between costing and performance. The system develops real-time scaling policies based on historical loads and company indicators. Tests indicate that efficiency is significantly increased with respect to the conventional methods.

title: M-GenCheck2: Fast Detection and Source Attribution of AI-Generated Images Using Deep Learning
authors: Nontawat Thongsibsong, Surapol Vorapatratorn
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence makes it easy to create photorealistic images, but it also raises concerns about trust, misinformation, and academic integrity. This paper presents M-GenCheck2, a convolutional neural network-based system for fast source attribution of AI-generated images. The model uses EfficientNet-B0 and is trained on a dataset of 3,205 AI-generated images collected from four major image generators, namely MidJourney, Stable Diffusion, DALL•E, and Flux, covering diverse prompts and visual styles. MGenCheck2 achieves 91.89% accuracy with an F1-score of 0.9189 on the training set and 66.86% accuracy with an F1-score of 0.6024 on an unseen test set, with relatively balanced precision and recall, indicating reasonable generalization for multi-class generator attribution under distribution shifts between training and unseen data. A resolution study shows that attribution performance improves with input quality and largely stabilizes at around <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 2 0}$</tex> pixels, while remaining usable at very low resolutions down to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 0}$</tex> pixels. Benchmarking against three popular online AI-image analysis services, Decopy.ai, Wasitai.com, and Isgen.ai, shows that M-GenCheck2 provides a practical trade-off between accuracy and latency, achieving 72.0% accuracy with an average end-to-end response time of 4.36 seconds per image. The system is deployed as a lightweight web application that provides real-time predictions and confidence scores, allowing unrestricted image submission without registration.

title: IoT-Integrated Smart Cold Storage Monitoring System with SaaS-Based Real-Time Analytics for Precision Agriculture
authors: Soumita Mandal, Ritam Mitra, Soham Sarkar, Tanmay Samanta, Krishna Karmakar, Anuj Dutta, Samit Karmakar, Soumik Kumar Kundu, Arunava Mukhopadhyay
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: In this paper, the authors demonstrate the IoTintegrated smart storage monitoring system intended to minimize or prevent post-harvest loss of produce and promote precision agriculture in small farmers. The prototype uses an ESP32 microcontroller with a DHT22, MQ4, and MQ135 sensors to continuously measure temperature & humidity, MQ135 methane concentration, and air quality in modular cold storage units. The real-time information is then relayed to a SaaS-based cloud platform via Wi-Fi, in addition to an LCD screen that delivers in-house feedback. It is evident, in this case, that the system proposed metal sensing, cost of which is low, and runs in rural single-phase power conditions.

title: Anomaly-Based Electricity Theft Detection and Reporting System
authors: C Harinakshi, G Suchetha, Sinchana Sheshagiri Naik, Shifa Kouser, Hanshitha Harimithran, P Roshan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Electricity theft is the main cause of Non-Technical Revenue (NTR) loss, causing significant financial challenges for power distribution companies, especially in developing countries. Traditional ways of finding theft, which rely on manual checks, are not efficient and cannot keep up with today’s needs. This paper introduces a complete, machine learning-based web app that can intelligently detect electricity theft. The main part of the system is an XGBoost model trained on past usage data to spot unusual patterns accurately. The app uses a separated design with a Next.js front end, a PostgreSQL database for storing data, and a Python Flask service that runs the model as part of a microservices setup. The app has two different user views: one for managing data and seeing reports, and another for customers to check their usage and report issues. This project shows a real-world, full solution that combines smart machine learning with modern web tools to cut down on NTR losses and create a fairer energy system.
