title: Design and Development of Vehicle Black Box System for Accident Analysis
authors: T Jayakumar, A Sabareeswaran, G A Sabarinath, M Tamil Selvan, D Sarathkumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The Design and Development of a Vehicle Black Box System aim to enhance accident analysis and improve road safety by leveraging IoT and advanced sensor technology. This system records critical data before, during, and after in incident, facilitating effective accident reconstruction and real-time monitoring. The system integrates various sensors and devices, such as alcohol and vibration sensors, a temperature sensor, and an ADXL 335 accelerometer, to monitor critical parameters. Equipped with GSM/GPS for real- time location tracking, a 32camera module for comprehensive visual data, and an LCD display for user interaction, the system provides detailed insights into accident scenarios. A PIC16F877A microcontroller acts as the system’s core, controlling components like relays, a motor, and a buzzer, ensuring seamless operation and reliable. The system integrates IoT technology to store and analyze data on the cloud, allowing stakeholders to access real-time updates and post-accidents reports. This innovation promotes safer driving, improves emergency responses, and provides valuable data for accident investigation and prevention.

title: Modular Design and Performance Metrics for Air Drives in Emergency Ventilator System
authors: Sushant Hanumantrao Shivankar
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Aerospace Engineering (ICMTAE)
abstract: In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, there has been a surge in the development of emergency ventilators, highlighting the need for resilient and adaptable designs. This paper proposes a modular approach to ventilator design, focusing on the “Air Drive” module-a key component that produces the necessary air or gas mixtures for mechanical ventilation. By standardizing this module, the design can improve supply chain resilience and simplify the integration of various air drive mechanisms. We explore different air drive mechanisms, including pressure-producing devices (fans, blowers, centrifugal pumps), volume-producing devices (positive displacement pumps, bag-mask valves, pistons, bellows), and pressure-releasing devices (electronically and fluidically controlled valves). A key aspect of the proposed design is its capability to produce power-on-theairway, which is the product of pressure and flow rate. The paper details the power requirements for effective ventilation, providing calculations for maximum power needs based on pressure and flow rates. It also addresses the integration of air drives into ventilators using control algorithms such as PID controllers to maintain desired ventilation modes (pressure control and volume control). Practical considerations for testing and implementing air drives are discussed, including performance metrics like power accuracy, response time, and reliability. Future work involves developing simulation models to verify the performance of modular air drives and ensuring their compatibility with different ventilator designs. The ultimate goal is to create a flexible, reliable, and easily maintainable ventilator system that can adapt to varying supply conditions and clinical needs.

title: Design and Simulation of a Wind-Fed ZVS Buck Converter with ZVS Bidirectional Battery Interface for DC Bus Voltage Regulation
authors: Souvik Mondal, Arnab Ghosh, Pravat Kumar Ray
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: This study describes the design and simulation of a hybrid wind energy conversion system that uses modern power electronics and energy storage to deal with the fact of wind generation changes all the time. A zero voltage (ZVS) buck converter is utilized to effectively extract energy from wind turbines while the wind is blowing in opposite directions. The MPPT algorithm, developed by P&O, controls it. Connecting a battery pack to the DC bus via a ZVS resonant bidirectional converter ensures a constant voltage and efficient power supply. This converter may either charge or discharge the battery depending on the current power balance between generation and consumption. It is managed by a proportional-integral (PI) controller. Modeling and design simulation for the suggested system was carried out using MATLAB. We can see that the MPPT is effective, that the conversion process is highly efficient with gentle switching, and that the power can be readily converted from the wind generator to the battery. Autonomous renewable energy systems can be made more reliable and efficient by combining ZVS converters with smart control strategies, according to the study.

title: An integrated graph-temporal generative and contrastive learning framework for fault detection in power networks
authors: Ishaan Basti, Sandeep Godhade, Priyanka Singh, Jayendra Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Modern power grids operate near stability limits and are sensitive to three-phase faults, asymmetrical ground faults, and low-frequency oscillations. Conventional deep learning can detect some of these events but struggles with limited topology awareness, data scarcity, and instability under changing conditions. We present a unified framework that combines graph-based feature embedding with a physics-guided GAN to capture coupling effects and generate rare fault patterns within physical limits. Contrastive multi-domain learning improves robustness across load profiles, while an attention-driven classifier with explainability identifies key buses and time windows. Self-calibration and online drift handling enable continuous adaptation to streaming data. Tests on IEEE 33, 39, and 118-bus systems show higher accuracy, better F1-scores for rare events, and lower latency and memory use than strong baselines, delivering an adaptive physics-aware solution for protection engineering.

title: Assessing Respiratory Disease Risk Through AI Driven Environmental Intelligence and Air Quality Modelling
authors: K. Madhavan, P. Thenmozhi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The impact of air pollution on the economy is unquestionable; due to having the highest level of exposureresponse functions, it is an impact on the Rates of of respiratory disease espinado etal. Therefore, the study of the relational dynamics between air pollution and respiratory disease should be one of the priority areas of research, where the relationship is undoubtedly bi-directional. In this research, we have developed a Machine Learning model using Random Forest regression for chronic respiratory disease prediction with the ambient air quality and weather attributes. The model consists of the weather and PM constituents ($\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}, \mathrm{PM}_{10}, \mathrm{NO}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}$) and achieved an RMSE of $16.22(16.22 / 100,000)$ and an R2 of 0.595. Out of the five independent variables ($\mathrm{PM}_{10}, \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, weather, and seasons), PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">10</inf>, weather and seasons were the most significant variables ($16.6 \%, 13.8 \%$, and 12.2%, respectively). The model is developed to stratify the populations as Low, Moderate, High, and Very High, which would be quite beneficial for the timely actions in the field of public health. The correlation of 0.808 between the AQI and the disease of the respiratory system strongly supports the prediction and relevance of the model to the environmental health decision support systems.

title: Symmetric Encryption-Decryption on Socket Programming File Transfer: DES, AES, and IDEA
authors: Aji Pangestu, Fauza Khair, Norma Amalia, Eko Fajar Cahyadi
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Socket programming is not equipped with an adequate security mechanism, making it vulnerable to wiretapping or data theft by irresponsible parties. To overcome this issue, a light cryptographic method is needed to guaranty the data transaction between the client and the server. Several symmetric cryptographic algorithms, such as data encryption standards, advanced encryption standards, and international data encryption algorithms, have advantages in terms of efficiency in encryption and message delivery, compared to asymmetric ones. However, to the best of our knowledge, the performance of the three algorithms is unknown when it comes to socket programming applications. In this study, an experiment was conducted by implementing the three algorithms in socket programming to find their performance. We send files in Txt, Docx, PDF, Rar, Jpg, Mp3, and MP4 formats, which were encrypted using the data encryption standard, advanced encryption standard, and international data encryption algorithm. From the results, it was found that the advanced encryption standard algorithm has the best performance in terms of encryption and decryption speed, so the time required is shorter. Meanwhile, the data encryption standard algorithm has an advantage in the size of the encrypted file, which does not increase in size.

title: Predicting Ten Year Cardiovascular Risk Using Framingham Heart Study Dataset
authors: Jash Ashar, Abhik Bangera, Aby Joseph, Adithya Girish, Akshita Choudhary, Aakriti Jain, T. R. Saravanan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Over the years, cardiovascular diseases continue to be a major contributor to global mortality all over the world, underscoring the need for reliable prediction models that support early detection and preventive care. This study employs the widely recognized Framingham Heart Study dataset, which serves as a foundational resource in cardiovascular risk modeling, to estimate the likelihood of developing coronary heart disease within a decade. The Orange Data Mining platform was used to design a complete workflow that included data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis, feature importance evaluation, and supervised learning models. Evaluation of the models was done through cross-validation and they were ranked using several performance metrics such as classification accuracy, positive predictive value, sensitivity, F1 measure, and the area under the ROC curve. Among the evaluated algorithms, Logistic Regression achieved the most balanced performance between accuracy and interpretability achieving $83 \%$ precision, $85 \%$ overall accuracy, an F1 measure of $0.80,85 \%$ recall, and an AUC value of 0.72. Based on our results, the features of importance were systolic blood pressure, age, whether the individual smokes and if the individual has diabetes and also suggests that Orange is an appropriate low-code tool for developing interpretable machine learning workflows.

title: Deploying Skycopter-X6 for Integrated Mapping, Inspection, and Emergency Response in Remote Terrains
authors: Dinesh K, Mahendran R, Geetha Rani K, Deepa R, Surendran R
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Due to poor monitoring infrastructure and inaccessibility, disaster-prone and remote terrains typically have issues with accurate mapping, timely inspection, and efficient emergency response. Traditional satellite and aerial picture systems are expensive, slow, and low-resolution. This research uses the Skycopter-X6, a high-tech unmanned aerial aircraft, and its engine, Task-Adaptive Resilient Navigation Algorithm (TARNA), to overcome these limitations. The TARNA framework combines MORL and MPC architectures. MORL prioritizes mapping, inspection, and emergency response based on mission urgency. Even in unexpected weather, MPC maintains navigation and energy efficiency. The technique performs many geospatial tasks using TARNA-coordinated real-time communication units, LiDAR, and multi-spectral imagery. Field testing in hilly and flood-affected areas demonstrated that the Skycopter-X6 reduced inspection time by 47%, reached mapping accuracy within a decimeter, and sent data continuously within 15 km. Task scheduling, route planning, and wind resilience were possible with TARNA. Finally, the Skycopter-X6 with TARNA is a costeffective smart aerial platform that can enhance geographic resilience, monitor infrastructure, and respond quickly to distant disasters.

title: A Dual Preference-Guided Constrained Optimization Algorithm Based on Pascoletti-Serafini
authors: Kuntai He, Linling Huang, Linzhen Li, Fangqing Gu
publish: 2025 21st International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Security (CIS)
abstract: Constrained optimization problems are ubiquitous in engineering design, resource scheduling, and financial decision making. However, current approaches face significant challenges in maintaining an effective balance between convergence, diversity, and feasibility. This paper introduces a dual preference-guided constrained optimization algorithm based on the Pascoletti-Serafini scalarization technique. Our approach transforms the objective space vectors into scalar values through an extended generalized Tchebycheff norm formulation. By integrating dynamically updated direction vectors with adaptive truncation parameters, the method enables guided exploration of the solution space while preserving both convergence efficiency and search diversity. A key innovation is the progressive constraint handling mechanism that adaptively increases selection pressure on constraint-violating solutions throughout the evolutionary process. This strategy effectively directs computational resources toward promising feasible regions as the algorithm evolves. Comprehensive experiments on 24 benchmark problems from the CEC2006 test suite demonstrate the superiority of our approach. The proposed algorithm exhibits robust performance in terms of solution feasibility and convergence stability, significantly enhancing MOEA/D's capability in solving constrained optimization problems. Results indicate consistent improvements across diverse problem characteristics, validating the effectiveness of integrating Pascoletti-Serafini scalarization with adaptive constraint handling.

title: A Feasibility Study on Analyzing the Effects of Onomatopoeic Features on Hand Motion
authors: Tomoo Kikuchi, Yuki Yoshinaga, Shoji Yamamoto, Eri Sato-Shimokawara
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Converting tacit knowledge of physical movement to explicit knowledge is a novel challenge in the field of computermediated contexts. This study investigates the relationship between physical motion and onomatopoeia, focusing on the effect of verbal information on actual behavior. We analyzed the correlation between onomatopoeia interpretation and physical gripping motions in experiments using a custom pressure sensor and questionnaires. Data from six participants were measured and analyzed via hierarchical clustering. As a result, inter-pretation-movement alignment increased with repetition, and normalized mutual information rose by 5.21 % on average across participants. By contrast, the overall correlation between the stability of onomatopoeia interpretations and the reproducibility of physical movements was low (maximum absolute Spearman correlation 0.354), and individual differences were large (sample standard deviation of improvement 3.40 %). Therefore, models should learn interpretation and bodily expression separately and then integrate them. These findings show that onomatopoeia to movement conversion systems must account for individual differences and dynamic learning processes.

title: Agentic Tool-Based Decision Agents for Finance: A Survey of Sentiment, Event, and Risk Integration
authors: S Renuka Devi, R Harsavardhini, S Kaviya, M Monisha
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Financial markets represent dynamic systems, heavily influenced by events around the world, many different types of macroeconomic indicators, and a constant flow of news information. Most financial analytics systems take either price history or static indicators as foundational input for their analyses. Such systems are poorly positioned to process contextually rich information in real time. The paper proposes an agent-based AI approach to the financial risk analysis implemented using Model Context Protocol that allows modular autonomous coordination of tools responsible for news ingestion, sentiment analysis, and computation of risks. The framework utilizes a domain-specific fine-tuning of the large language model, Gemma-2B via Ollama, on the FinancialPhraseBank dataset to locally make the financial sentiment inference. The sentiment outputs are then transformed into a Dynamic Risk Index, while the Portfolio Risk Index is derived by aggregating these individual risk scores. The agentic workflow is demonstrated through an executable MCP pipeline which processes financial news and produces explainable, quantitative risk indicators. Experimental results on real-world news streams confirm the viability of the proposed system and demonstrate the delivery of interpretable, adaptive, and reproducible financial risk insights enabled by agentic tool orchestration combined with fine-tuned language models.

title: Semantic Web Healthcare Chatbot Using Ontology and Data Mining for Patient Information Assistance
authors: Chitiz Tayal, Sujit Murumkar, Brij Mohan
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: This work uses ontology engineering and data mining to create and deploy a Semantic Web healthcare chatbot to enhance patient information support systems, which are becoming more crucial in digital healthcare. Healthcare organisations may use the Semantic Web to handle massive amounts of machinereadable and intelligible medical data. Semantically enhanced knowledge representations and automated analytic methods increase chatbot accuracy, relevance, and contextual comprehension, reducing rule-based systems' inconsistent outputs. This topic uses ontologies, linked data, and semantic web technologies to improve healthcare data interoperability and enable the accurate interchange of medical knowledge, records, clinical advice, and research. Over 64% of patients prefer automated conversational interfaces for preliminary medical guidance, and nearly 72% of healthcare organisations have adopted AI-enabled triage or support systems, indicating a growing societal reliance on automated health information platforms. Ontological analysis improves information comprehension. Hospital information management and patient self-service systems benefit from semantic chatbots' 89% retrieval accuracy and 43% reduction in irrelevant response production. An ontology is the foundation of any domain-specific knowledge representation system that removes data inconsistencies to assure validity.

title: Assessment of Cardiomegaly from Chest X-Ray Using Deep Learning Artificial Intelligence
authors: Jiraphat Thongyoo, Suradej Intagorn, Punyanuch Borwarnginn, Thanongchai Siriapisith, Worapan Kusakunniran
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
abstract: Chest radiography is essential in assessing heart size and shape, contributing to the screening of lung and heart diseases. The cardiothoracic ratio (CTR), defined as the ratio of the heart's largest transverse dimension to the chest's largest transverse dimension, serves as a crucial indicator of cardiac abnormalities. Traditional CTR measurement relies on segmentation-based approaches, which are prone to inaccuracies due to anatomical variations and inconsistencies in image quality. This study introduces a novel regression-based deep learning approach for automated CTR prediction, leveraging ProGAN-generated synthetic chest X-ray data. The proposed method eliminates the dependency on segmentation while enhancing accuracy and efficiency. A CNN model is developed and trained on ProGAN-generated images. The effectiveness of the regression approach is compared with conventional segmentationbased techniques, with accuracy assessments and Grad-CAM visualizations. The findings demonstrate improved performance and robustness in CTR prediction, offering a potential screening tool to assist medical professionals in assessing cardiomegaly.

title: Skin Disease Diagnosis by using MobileNetV3Large
authors: G Sunil, Shanvi Chauhan, Saurabh Fulara, Chandra Kishore, Neha Sharma
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Early and precise diagnosis of skin diseases is a crucial factor in successful patient care and clinical treatment. This paper contains a deep learning framework of multiclassification of dermoscopic images into three classes: Eczema, Atopic Dermatitis, and Seborrheic Keratoses with other benign tumors. A total of $\mathbf{3, 8 6 3}$ dermoscopic images are used and stratified at the ratio of $70: 15: 15$ to produce 2,705 training images, 580 validation images, and 578 testing ones. In order to further improve model generalization and decrease overfitting, the data augmentation methods like horizontal flipping and brightness variation are utilized, and the MobileNetV3Large is used to preprocess the data. The convergence of the model proposed is stable and has little divergence of the training curve and validation curve. The results of the experiments show that the overall test accuracy is 92 with equal precision, recall and $\mathbf{F 1}$-scores over all classes. The confusion matrix shows high levels of diagonal dominance, which shows that there is solid discrimination between visual similar skin conditions. These results underscore the usefulness of the suggested solution to automated skin disease screening and its possibility to be incorporated into the clinical decisionsupport systems in practice.

title: Hardware Software Co-Design of a CNN Accelerator for Real Time Image Classification
authors: Shardul Penurkar, Sangeeta Nakhate, Gaurav Tiwari
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have become the cornerstone of modern image recognition tasks, demonstrating remarkable accuracy in applications ranging from digit recognition to complex object detection. However, their computational requirements pose significant challenges for real-time deployment on resource-constrained embedded systems. This paper presents a comprehensive hardware-software co-design approach for accelerating CNN inference on the MNIST dataset, targeting the ZCU102 FPGA platform. The CNN model was initially trained in software using TensorFlow, achieving high accuracy on the test set, and its weights were subsequently exported and integrated into a hardware design implemented in Vitis HLS. The design employs custom hardware modules for convolution, activation, pooling, and fully connected layers, interfaced through BRAM with a Verilog testbench for validation. Simulation and synthesis results indicate substantial improvement in inference latency compared to software-only execution, while maintaining efficient utilization of FPGA resources. Detailed analysis of LUT, BRAM, and DSP utilization highlights the scalability and potential for extending the design to larger datasets and deeper CNN architectures, making it suitable for real-time embedded AI applications.

title: Fourier Transform-based instance decomposition for -adic Assignment Problems_supp1-3674527.pdf
authors: Xabier Benavides
abstract: In combinatorial optimization, the Fourier Transform (FT) on the symmetric group has been used to analyze and optimize permutation-based problems. However, the difficulties that arise when working within Fourier space have limited its potential in practical scenarios. To address this issue, rather than using the FT to decompose the objective function, an alternative possibility is to use the FT as a tool to break down the instance defining the problem at hand. This strategy captures the information from each Fourier coefficient as a separate instance of the same problem, resulting in a potentially more usable setting in the context of optimization. So far, the FT-based instance decomposition has been limited to a small sample of problems characterized by low-order Fourier coefficients. In this work, we generalize this notion to a much broader class of problems, known as k-adic Assignment Problems (k-APs). We present a general framework for the decomposition of k-AP instances and discuss its practical applications. As a possible use case, we propose an instance generation method that exploits the FT-based instance decomposition to create artificial instances with tunable difficulty levels.

title: UAV classification using Attentive Binarized CNN for micro-Doppler spectrograms
authors: Ipsita Paul, Mainak Bandyopadhyay
abstract: Radar-based micro-Doppler spectrograms offer a robust representation for classifying aerial objects, particularly small Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), in scenarios where optical sensing is limited or infeasible. However, deploying deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for this task on edge devices remains challenging due to their high computational and memory demands. In this work, we have proposed a hybrid approach, for creating a compact and energy-efficient neural architecture. This is done by integrating binarization and attention modules for enhancing representational capacity along with significant reduction in model complexity. Experimental evaluations on the DIAT-μSAT dataset demonstrate good classification performance with 97.83% accuracy on our proposed QA_MVGG_10 architecture. We have further validated on another dataset RRN_ATR_NET, which confirms the model’s robustness, i.e., cross-dataset generalization, by achieving 99.17% accuracy. This work contributes to a scalable and interpretable solution for on-device GPU inference that can be further extended to radar sensing on edge-deployable systems. This bridges the gap between model efficiency and classification fidelity in modern defense and surveillance applications.

title: Wi-Depth: Reconstructing Depth Images of Moving Objects from Wi-Fi CSI Data
authors: Guanyu Cao, Kazuya Ohara, Yasue Kishino, Takuya Maekawa
abstract: This study proposes a new deep learning method for reconstructing depth images of moving objects within a specific area using Wi-Fi channel state information (CSI). The Wi-Fibased depth imaging technique has novel applications in domains such as security and elder care. However, reconstructing depth images from CSI is challenging because it is difficult to learn the mapping function between CSI and depth images, both of high-dimensionalities. To address the challenge, we propose Wi-Depth. The main idea behind the design of Wi-Depth is that a depth image of a moving object can be decomposed into three core components: the shape, depth, and position of the target. Therefore, in the depth-image reconstruction task, Wi-Depth simultaneously estimates the three core pieces of information as auxiliary tasks in our proposed VAE-based teacher-student architecture, enabling it to output images with the consistency of a correct shape, depth, and position. In addition, the design of Wi-Depth is based on our idea that this decomposition efficiently takes advantage of the fact that shape, depth, and position relate to primitive information inferred from CSI such as angle-of-arrival, time-of-flight, and Doppler frequency shift.

title: Performance-Balanced Task Allocation in Leader-Member Teams: A Group Multirole Assignment Approach Using E-CARGO
authors: Jiahui Yu, Xinlei Zhang, Lisha Peng, Haibin Zhu, Yuxiang Sun, Xianzhong Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Role-based collaboration (RBC) is a role-centered computational paradigm for solving collaborative problems, where group multirole assignment (GMRA) is an important component. This article focuses on leader–member teams, a common organizational structure in project management, and extends the GMRA framework to address two critical challenges. First, evaluating the qualifications of leaders and members is nontrivial due to their distinct responsibilities. To address this, we propose a capability–requirement matching evaluation (CRME) method that applies differentiated mechanisms to assess leaders and members. Second, existing studies mainly maximize overall performance while neglecting task performance balance, which is vital for synchronized progress. To overcome this limitation, we develop a group multirole assignment with balanced task performance (GMRABP) model that incorporates a penalty-augmented objective to maximize team performance while reducing disparities across tasks. Furthermore, two linearized variants, GMRABP-A and GMRABP-B, are introduced to enhance computational efficiency. Extensive experiments and comparative analyses validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods, offering practical strategies for managing projects where both performance maximization and progress coordination are essential.

title: A μ-Synthesis Framework for Multi-Domain Robust Load Frequency Control under Concurrent Communication Delays and Parametric Uncertainties
authors: Chadi Nohra, Bechara Nehme, Raymond Ghandour, Ossama Dankar, Mahmoud Khaled, Ahmed Mohsin Alsayah
abstract: The integration of communication networks into modern power systems introduces variable time delays that degrade the performance of traditional Load Frequency Control (LFC), while the shift towards renewable energy sources increases system vulnerability through parametric uncertainties. Existing methods, predominantly based on Lyapunov-Krasovskii Functionals, involve a complexity–conservatism trade-off and may not provide a unified and tractable solution for this multi-domain robustness challenge. This paper addresses this gap by proposing a novel control framework based on μ-analysis. The methodology models communication delays as structured uncertainties using a Padé approximation and integrates them with parametric variations within a unified μ-synthesis design process. A detailed comparative analysis indicates that unlike Lyapunov-based approaches, which require guaranteeing system smoothness at every delay subinterval, the proposed method efficiently stabilizes the system under the worst-case conditions, quantified by the structured singular value. Simulation results demonstrate improved robustness compared to conventional H∞ control under concurrent delay and parametric uncertainties. While the conventional H∞ controller exhibits degraded stability margins when delays exceed 15 ms, the proposed μ-synthesis controller maintains stability and performance under extreme concurrent disturbances, including time-varying delays of 0.5–5 s, 40% load changes, and over 80% variation in tie-line reactance and turbine-governor time constants. The proposed controller drives the Area Control Error (ACE) below 0.01 pu within two minutes for a 40% load change under these conditions. These results indicate that μ-analysis provides a systematic framework for achieving multi-domain robustness in Load Frequency Control under large simultaneous uncertainties.

title: Lightweight Multi-Layer Security for Internet of Things Applications
authors: Piyush Bhatt, Saksham Singh, Prerana Mitra, Soham Kanti Bishnu, Arindam Chakraborty
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: The expansion of the Internet of Things introduces devices that use limited resources and operate with low power. These devices communicate data across networks that provide limited security. Traditional approaches to encryption require substantial computation, and this creates problems for devices with constrained resources. The devices show vulnerability to attacks that include eavesdropping, impersonation, cloning, and real-time attacks. This work addresses these limitations. The work presents a framework that provides security through multiple layers. The framework integrates an approach using DNA-based methods for encryption that require limited computation. This approach combines with Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC). Four additional protection layers are followed by this - comprising of (i) device authentication, (ii) hardware-level identity using Physical Unclonable Functions (PUF), (iii) transport security through TLS/DTLS, and (iv) anomaly detection using AI. The use of DNA-based biological randomness integrates with the above-mentioned four additional protection layers to provide holistic and advanced-level security for resource-limited IoT de-vices while maintaining a low computational cost. The approach explained here ensures confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and prevention of realtime threats faced in day-to-day life in a consolidated design. The system proposed here illustrates a novel and effective direction for the upcoming generation of lightweight IoT reliability.

title: Complementary Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Adversarial Robustness_supp1-3669252.pdf
authors: Lu Yu
abstract: Due to the impressive zero-shot capabilities, pre-trained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), have attracted widespread attention and adoption across various domains. Nonetheless, CLIP has been observed to be susceptible t adversarial examples. Through experimental analysis, we have observed a phenomenon wherein adversarial perturbations induce shifts in text-guided attention. Building upon this observation, we propose a simple yet effective strategy: Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Robustness (TGA-ZSR). This framework incorporates two components: Local Attention Refinement Module and Global Attention Constraint Module. Our goal is to maintain the generalization of the CLIP model and enhance its adversarial robustness: The Local Attention Refinement Module aligns the text-guided attention obtained from the target model via adversarial examples with the text-guided attention acquired from the original model via clean examples. This alignment enhances the model's robustness. Additionally, the Global Attention Constraint Module acquires text-guided attention from both the target and original models using clean examples. Its objective is to maintain model performance on clean samples while enhancing overall robustness. However, we observe that the method occasionally focuses on irrelevant or spurious features, which can lead to suboptimal performance and undermine its robustness in certain scenarios. To overcome this limitation, we further propose a novel approach called Complementary Text-Guided Attention (Comp-TGA). This method integrates two types of foreground attention: attention guided by the class prompt and reversed attention driven by the non-class prompt. These complementary attention mechanisms allow the model to capture a more comprehensive and accurate representation of the foreground. The experiments validate that TGA-ZSR and Comp-TGA yield 9.58% and 11.95% improvements respectively, in zero-shot robust accuracy over the current state-of-the-art techniques across 16 datasets.

title: Formation Control of Stochastic Heterogeneous Multiagent Systems: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions
authors: Kewei Zhang, Jiazheng Zhang, Erchao Li
abstract: This article studies the formation control of stochastic heterogeneous multiagent systems (HMASs) subject to compound (additive and multiplicative) noises and directed topologies. Due to the intrinsic coupling between the directed topologies and heterogeneous dynamics, classical Lyapunov-based stability techniques become inapplicable. Therefore, we introduce a velocity-decomposition technique and a semidecoupling method to address the formation control of stochastic HMASs. First, the boundedness of agents’ states is established using properties of Hurwitz matrices. Then, by applying the Jordan decomposition of the system matrix, some convergence conditions of the closed-loop system are derived. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to study the formation control of stochastic HMASs under compound noises and directed topologies and to derive necessary and sufficient conditions for achieving the desired formation. Finally, numerical simulations and physical experiments are given to validate theoretical results.

title: LunarRockSAM: A Domain-Adapted SAM with Bright-Spots Prompting and Conditional Screening for Lunar Rock Extraction
authors: Yinan Wang, Hongxia Ye, Wenzhe Fa
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate extraction of lunar rocks and analysis of their size distribution are essential for lunar rover navigation and geological investigations. However, previous methods often suffer from inaccurate boundary delineation for irregularly shaped rocks, and poor performance in complex terrain regions. In this study, we propose an enhanced segmentation anything model (SAM) framework, which integrates optical luminance and gradient variations as prior knowledge to improve lunar rock extraction (LunarRockSAM). The SAM mask decoder is fine-tuned on a manually labeled lunar rock dataset to improve adaption to lunar surface characteristics and significantly enhance segmentation precision. A novel bright-spots extraction algorithm is proposed to generate point prompts for accurate rock localization, reducing miss detections in complex terrain regions. Furthermore, a conditional screening mechanism is incorporated to refine segmentation masks for higher accuracy. Our method achieves pixel-level segmentation results with an average precision of 90% and a recall of 86%. Applied to Chang'e-3 (CE-3) and Chang'e-5 (CE-5) landing sites, the proposed method successfully counts rocks with diameters between 0.1 m and 7.17 m. The results show that the CE-3 site has a higher rock density, smaller sizes (0.1–2.72 m), and a steeper slope in size-frequency distribution curve, while the CE-5 site exhibits lower rock density and larger rocks (1.0–7.17 m). Based on these size and spatial distributions, we conducted a detailed comparative analysis of the geological characteristics and differences between the two regions, providing a valuable support for autonomous rover navigation and lunar geological investigation.

title: Analysis of Stress Levels from Lifestyle and Health Factors: A Comparison of OLS and GLM Approaches
authors: Sarbatriki Jana, Saraddyuti Chakravarty, Monali Das
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: In today's world, stress is one of the major issues of concern. Long-term stress can harm sleep, daily tasks, productivity, and long-term health. Because of this, the prediction and measurement of the level of stress have become important in health analytics, especially with the readily available health and lifestyle data from digital devices and wearable. Researchers often rely on statistical modeling to explain how physiological and behavioral signals relate to stress. These models range from simple linear techniques to more advanced predictive frameworks. Regression-based methods are often the standard way of analyzing many predictors in an organized manner. In this study, two widely used such methods have been compared using a synthetic dataset. The two models are Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) and the Generalized Linear Model (GLM). The results will help to understand which of these regression frameworks provide better robustness and reliable predictions under structured data. It will further help to relate the expected behavior of these models to real-world health data.

title: Air Pollution Classification Using a Hybrid Attention-Enabled Deep Learning Model
authors: Periasamy Sannasi, Subramanian P, Surendran R
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: Air Globally, factors such as urbanization and technological evolution have been considered the cause for the degradation of the environment. One of the factors considered as the biggest problems in the metro areas globally is the problem of pollution in the atmosphere. The problem is especially prevalent in the Indian capital, Delhi, where the health problems associated with environmental pollution affect the people as well as the government. So, this study tests the six-year data for the 23 Indian cities regarding the classification of the levels of environmental pollution in the atmosphere. The data is initially properly pre-processed to remove the noise in the data. Finally, a hybrid deep learning (DL) model, named Temporal Convolution and Attention-enabled Bi-directional Long Short Term Memory (TCoABi-LSTM) is proposed to perform the classification of air pollution level. Overall, the proposed framework is implemented using the Python platform and analysed by different performance parameters that shows 97.98 % classification accuracy and 96 % precision on the utilized dataset.

title: Impact of Dataset Size and Class Diversity on ResNet Performance
authors: Diya Singh, Bhavya Kumar, Harshita Ranai, Dixita, Harshita Rawat, Himanshu Mittal
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Electronics, Materials Engineering &amp; Nano-Technology (IEMENTech)
abstract: In this study, we systematically investigate the impact of statically changing the dataset size and class diversity of the ResNet model to evaluate its performance. A Convolutional Neural Network is prominently used for image classification tasks. The experiments are conducted on the CIFAR-10 dataset, which is a standard benchmark for evaluating CNN models. The primary objective of this paper is to analyze the impact of varying the number of selected training samples and the range of classes included in the dataset to calculate models' performance using Accuracy and Macro F1 as standard parameters. We will take subsets of varying ranges and assign them with equal samples per class to check the effects of dataset size and class diversity on ResNet's ability to maintain high performance on unseen data.

title: Prompt-Driven Low-Altitude Edge Intelligence: Modular Agents and Generative Reasoning
authors: Jiahao You, Ziye Jia, Chao Dong, Qihui Wu
abstract: The large artificial intelligence models (LAMs) show strong capabilities in perception, reasoning, and multi-modal understanding, and can enable advanced capabilities in low-altitude edge intelligence. However, the deployment of LAMs at the edge remains constrained by some fundamental limitations. First, tasks are rigidly tied to specific models, limiting the flexibility. Besides, the computational and memory demands of full-scale LAMs exceed the capacity of most edge devices. Moreover, the current inference pipelines are typically static, making it difficult to respond to real-time changes of tasks. To address these challenges, we propose a prompt-to-agent edge cognition framework (P2AECF), enabling the flexible, efficient, and adaptive edge intelligence. Specifically, P2AECF transforms high-level semantic prompts into executable reasoning workflows through three key mechanisms. First, the prompt-defined cognition parses task intent into abstract and model-agnostic representations. Second, the agent-based modular execution instantiates these tasks using lightweight and reusable cognitive agents dynamically selected based on current resource conditions. Third, the diffusion-controlled inference planning adaptively constructs and refines execution strategies by incorporating runtime feedback and system context. In addition, we illustrate the framework through a representative low-altitude intelligent network use case, showing its ability to deliver adaptive, modular, and scalable edge intelligence for real-time low-altitude aerial collaborations.

title: Multigroup Multicast Design for Pinching-Antenna Systems: Waveguide-Division or Waveguide-Multiplexing?
authors: Shan Shan, Chongjun Ouyang, Yong Li, Yuanwei Liu
abstract: This article addresses the design of multigroup multicast communications in the pinching-antenna system (PASS). A PASS-enabled multigroup transmission framework is proposed to maximize multicast rates under a couple of transmission architectures: waveguide-division (WD) and waveguide-multiplexing (WM). 1) For WD, an element-wise sequential optimization strategy is proposed for pinching beamforming, i.e., optimizing the activated positions of pinching antennas along dielectric waveguides. Meanwhile, a log-sum-exp projected gradient descent algorithm is proposed for transmit power allocation across waveguides. 2) For WM, a majorization-minimization (MM)-based framework is proposed to tackle the problem’s non-smoothness and non-convexity. On this basis, a low-complexity element-wise sequential optimization method is developed for pinching beamforming using the MM surrogate objective. Furthermore, the optimal transmit beamformer structure is derived from the MM surrogate objective using the Lagrange duality, with an efficient transmit beamforming algorithm proposed using projected adaptive gradient descent. Numerical results demonstrate that: i) both WD and WM architectures in PASS achieve significant multicast rate improvements over conventional MIMO techniques, especially for systems with large service areas; ii) WM is more robust than WD in dense deployments, while WD excels when user groups are spatially separated.

title: Two-Stage Topology Identification for Radial Medium-Voltage Distribution Network with Voltage Magnitude-Only Measurements
authors: Xue Li, Junlin Yang, Zhe Zhou, Yaqi Qian, Dajun Du, Lei Wu
abstract: Due to limited sensor deployment and measurement capabilities in medium-voltage (MV) distribution networks, voltage magnitude measurements are readily accessible and used to design single-stage topology identification methods, leading to low accuracy in topology identification without considering the inherent structural characteristics of power grid. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel two-stage topology identification method for radial MV distribution network with voltage magnitude-only measurements. According to historical voltage magnitude measurements, the first stage establishes a linear regression model with an L1 norm penalty term, which is solved by using Lasso method to obtain the initially identified topological structure. To eliminate redundancy in the initial topology, the second stage proposes a novel neighbor set updating strategy and an improved OR rule for branch recovery based on the correlation coefficients and structural characteristics, achieving more accurate topology identification. Experimental results confirm the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Fine-Tuned EfficientNetB0 Model for the Drone vs. Bird Detection Using a YOLO-Based Segmented Dataset
authors: Gotte Ranjith Kumar, Ritu Rani, Sonal Malhotra, Kamlesh Kukreti, Neha Sharma
publish: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics and Computer Science (SCEECS)
abstract: The rapid increase in the application of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) has raised serious security concerns due in large part to their inability to sort out the presence of drones versus birds in the aerial space. It proposes a deep-learning-based approach with one of the segmentation datasets based on the YOLO framework specifically created to accomplish this purpose to overcome this challenge through precise drone vs. bird detection. The dataset has over 20,000 labeled photos in different environmental contexts, and thus reinforces model resilience. The model employed in this work was a finely tuned EfficientNetB0 model, with transfer learning being applied to obtain high-level features with good efficiency at a cost-effective computation. The proposed model with the classification accuracy of 99, preciseness of 100 and F1-score of 99 is incredibly effective to reduce the false classification of drones and birds. An additional reason to support the reliability of the model is an in-depth evaluation based on a confusion matrix analysis. In comparative study on recent research, our method outperforms many of the existing models such as MobileNetV2, CNN, YOLOv9-AAG, and MobVGG. The current work confirms the existence of a viable, real-time drone detection system based on a compact yet powerful model of EfficientNetB0, hence significantly enhancing the aerial surveillance and security systems.

title: A Multimode Built-In Self-Test Circuit for Sequential Cell Timing Characteristics Based on Digital-to-Time Converter
authors: Wenwen Cai, Yanhui Zhao, Zhengrui Chen, Pan Zheng, Yang Cui, Wei Zou, Xuecheng Zou, Cheng Zhuo, Li Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: To address the growing challenges in high-precision timing characterization, where conventional measurement methods face limitations due to increasing process variations and multicharacteristic testing demands, this work presents an innovative multimode built-in self-test (BIST) circuit based on a digital-to-time converter (DTC). This circuit comprises a multimode design under test (DUT) module, a DTC, and a controller, enabling the simultaneous measurement of critical timing characteristics (setup/hold times and CK–Q/QN delays) across multiple sequential cells with varying trigger types and clock edges. To enhance the measurement performance, we propose a DTC architecture featuring a digitally controlled multilevel tunable delay cell within a two-stage “coarse–fine” delay chain and a multilevel switching mechanism. The BIST circuit achieves a minimum resolution of 18.7 ps, with the DTC offering a 10.5-ns dynamic range and high linearity, occupying 0.03 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, while the total BIST area is 0.58 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Experimental results in a 180-nm process show setup/hold time measurement errors of −21 to 27 and −27 to 25 ps, respectively, and a CK–Q(QN) delay error range of −22.3 to 30.7 ps.

title: Safety-Constrained Control with Tunable Performance Using Dual-Mode Method and Local Trajectory Adjustment
authors: Xingqiang Zhao, Yongduan Song, Changyun Wen
abstract: This paper presents a novel safety-constrained control framework for a class of uncertain high-order multi-input multi-output (MIMO) nonlinear systems, unifying trajectory correction strategies with tunable performance constraint designs. In contrast to existing methods that assume the desired trajectory is always safe and feasible, we introduce a localized trajectory correction mechanism based on a compact-support transition function. This mechanism achieves intended adjustments by superimposing local correction terms onto the reference trajectory. Additionally, we propose a new dual-mode tunable performance function that dynamically adapts to real-time tracking errors. Notably, this design allows users to predefine relaxation or tightening intervals to address potential perturbations or safety requirements. By constructing a barrier-inspired function through normalization mapping, the constrained system is transformed into an unconstrained one. Stabilizing this transformed system effectively solves the original constrained control problem. Simulation results demonstrate the applicability and effectiveness of the proposed framework, showcasing its advantages in handling system uncertainties and ensuring performance constraints.

title: Online Anti-Jamming Decision-Making for Frequency-Agile Radar in Non-Stationary Environments
authors: Hongyu Zhu, Rong Xie, Zheng Liu, Shuwen Xu, Lei Ran
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: In electronic warfare environments, online anti jamming decision-making for frequency-agile radars must cope with unknown adversaries, limited interaction budgets, and non stationarity, where standard reinforcement learning can be sample inefficient and slow to adapt. We propose a Posterior Sampling for Reinforcement Learning (PSRL)–centric online decision frame work with dual-channel non-stationarity detection on rewards and transitions under a non-stationary Markov decision process (MDP) model. On the reward side, a Bernoulli-mean confidence-bound test is used to detect shifts in detection outcomes; on the transition side, a L1-deviation statistic is applied to empirical multinomial transition distributions. Detection thresholds are derived in closed form and explicitly tied to a target false-alarm probability, enabling principled parameter tuning. When either channel signals a change, the corresponding Bayesian posterior parameters are reset to discard stale information and accelerate adaptation to the new regime. Simulations across multiple stationary and non-stationary jamming scenarios evaluate convergence speed, recovery time, recovery rate, and cumulative return, and include sensitivity analyses over window length and false-alarm level to quantify the trade-off among detection sensitivity, delay, and false alarms. Results show that in stationary environments the detector remains inactive and preserves PSRL's sample efficiency, while in non-stationary environments the proposed method achieves faster recovery and improved steady state performance, yielding sustained gains in cumulative return.

title: Efficient Arbitrary-scale Super-Resolution with Compact Gaussian Splatting
authors: Jingyi Zhang, Haonan Jiang, Jiajun Dong, Shuai Shen, Yansong Tang, Lei Chen, Jiwen Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Arbitrary-scale super-resolution is an essential image upsampling task, typically tackled with implicit neural representations. Unlike INR-based methods that rely on slow per-pixel decoding, Gaussian Splatting is promising for arbitrary-scale super-resolution when the explicit region-based nature of GS allows for highly efficient rendering via a lightweight decoder. Recently, Gaussian splatting has outperformed implicit neural methods in 3D scenes. However, existing attempts to address the super-resolution problem with Gaussian splatting face efficiency and accuracy challenges, such as redundant Gaussian primitives and discrete pixel sampling. Efficient Gaussian applications may result in continuous texture constraints due to limited feature richness in explicit fields, particularly the mismatch between the learned Gaussian fields and out-of-distribution sampling rates. Moreover, insufficient discrete sampling based on the given upscale factor may fail to accurately represent the splatted Gaussian field in screen space, causing high-frequency signal redundancy and aliasing. To address these challenges, we propose a compact Gaussian splatting method for efficient arbitrary-scale super-resolution, CGSSR. It constructs an efficient Gaussian space by distilling the image into a reduced number of primitives, each represented by a compact, low-dimensional feature embedding. To balance detail preservation and anti-aliasing, we introduce a scale-aware smoothing filter to regulate splatting frequency. Extensive experiments show that CGSSR achieves superior performance over existing Gaussian-based methods, especially at large scales, with higher efficiency.

title: Development and testing of forced-flow cooling CORC Joint
authors: Ming Li, Jinxing Zheng, Tao Ma, Xiongyi Huang, Yuan Cheng, Yangyang Shi
abstract: Superconducting joint is the key component for the superconducting magnet system, which determines the ohmic heat loading during high current carrying operation conditions. Tapered structure is usually used in CORC joint, which can realize the direct electrical contact between each REBCO layer and copper plate. However, when considering forced-flow cooling, traditional tapered structure needs to be optimized to realize cooling medium transmission and enhance current distribution uniformity. A new CORC joint with forced-flow cooling method is introduced in this paper. The relationship between tapered length of each layer and current distribution unevenness is also analyzed, it reveals that tapered length is greater than twisting pitch length of CORC cable, the current distribution uniformity is acceptable. For transporting cooling medium (liquid helium, supercritical helium and so on), there is two cooling channel, including central cooling channel and circumferential cooling channel, which can realize efficient transmission of central and annular cooling medium (The central core shaft with spiral holes can realize the exchange of cooling medium between the center and the annular direction). The forced-flow CORC joint has been prepared and 77 nΩ resistance has been achieved. 1.5 K temperature rise occurs with 4 kA current. In the updated design, the copper head size is further increased and cooling channels are added. The resistance achieved 46 nΩ and only 0.5 K temperature rise occurs with 4 kA current carrying. The developed CORC joint has been used on the HTS SMES system with maximum 10 kA current capacity.
